Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

About This Book
This single-volume edition consolidates the ten-volume Garda Investigation Techniques series into one
operational reference. It is designed for the reference moment - when the member needs the framework at hand
for the case in front of them, not for cover-to-cover reading.

How to Navigate
Master Table of Contents (next pages) - volume and chapter-level listing of the whole book.
Topic Index - alphabetical lookup of common operational topics with Volume:Chapter references.
Case Law Index - alphabetical lookup of every case treated in vLex depth across the series.
Statute Index - alphabetical lookup of every statute referenced operationally.
Each volume opens with its own Front Matter explaining the volume's structure and core concepts.
Each volume's appendices include Defences and Practice, Evidence and Admissibility, Statutory Anchors, and
Case Law in vLex depth.
Each chapter cross-references other volumes where related topics appear.

The Ten Volumes - At a Glance
Vol

Topic

Principal Coverage

1

Property Crime

Theft, burglary, robbery, fraud, criminal damage

2

Homicide, NFOAP assaults, threats, endangerment, false imprisonment

4

DV Act 2018, coercive control s.39, protective orders, DV homicide

5

Drugs

MDA framework, possession, supply, s.15A threshold, production, powers

6

Rape, sexual assault, consent doctrine, child offences, specialist

7

RTA framework, drink/drug driving, testing procedure, fatal collision

8

Sudden death, missing persons, mental health s.12 MHA, family liaison

9

Cross-Cutting
Reference

Weapons, comms data, PULSE/PEMS, command, custody, disclosure

10

Intelligence cycle, grading, HUMINT/OSINT/surveillance/financial/forensic,
building and use

The Working Reference Principle
Across all ten volumes, the same disclaimer operates: this is a working reference, not legal advice. Irish
statutory law, case law, and Garda operational policy continue to develop. Before relying on any statement here
for an operational decision, verify on revisedacts.lawreform.ie, irishstatutebook.ie, vLex Ireland, BAILII,
HUDOC (ECtHR), or curia.europa.eu (CJEU) as appropriate. Where Garda HQ directives or operational policy
apply, those take precedence. Where doubt exists, supervisor consultation is the operational standard.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

MASTER TABLE OF CONTENTS

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 1 - Property Crime
Front Matter - How to Use; The Property Crime Operational Framework; Core Mnemonics.
Ethics - Property Crime Application; Trip-Wires.
Ch 1 - Theft (s.4 Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001).
Ch 2 - Burglary (s.12 CJ(TaFO)A 2001).
Ch 3 - Aggravated Burglary / Robbery.
Ch 4 - Fraud / Deception offences.
Ch 5 - Handling stolen property / money laundering.
Ch 6 - Criminal damage (Criminal Damage Act 1991).
Appendices - Defences and Practice; Evidence; Statutory Anchors; Case Law (vLex); Back Matter.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 2 - Crimes Against Person
Front Matter - operational framework, mnemonics, escalation continuum.
Ethics - person-crime ethics, trip-wires.
Ch 1 - Homicide (murder s.4 CJA 1964, manslaughter).
Ch 2 - Assault offences (s.2/3/4 NFOAP 1997).
Ch 3 - Threats and harassment (s.5 NFOAP threats to kill; s.10 harassment).
Ch 4 - Endangerment, false imprisonment, abduction (s.13/15/16 NFOAP).
Appendices - Defences (self-defence, provocation, intoxication); Evidence; Statutory Anchors; Case Law
(foundational procedural authorities including MacEoin, Davis, Heffernan, Dwyer, Murray, Walsh, Madden,
Buck, Casey, Gormley, Doyle, JC, Damache, Tiernan); Back Matter.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 3 - Public Order
Front Matter - POA 1994 architecture, mnemonics.
Ethics - public order ethics, discretion.
Ch 1 - Foundational POA offences (s.5/6/8/9).
Ch 2 - Directions and obligations (s.7/8).
Ch 3 - Group / violent disorder offences (s.14/15).
Ch 4 - Property-adjacent offences (s.10/11/12/13).
Ch 5 - Specialist / aggravated (s.16/17).
Ch 6 - Adjacent (s.39 CJ(MP)A 2023 stalking; CJ(PO)A 1994 trespass; blackmail s.17 CJ(PO)A).
Appendices - Defences and Practice; Statutory Anchors; Case Law (vLex); Back Matter.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 4 - Domestic Violence
Front Matter - DV operational framework, lethality markers, four mnemonics (DASH, SAFE, STOP,
RECORD).
Ethics - DV-specific ethics, trip-wires, trauma dimension.
Ch 1 - DV Act 2018 architecture.
Ch 2 - Coercive control s.39 DV Act 2018.
Ch 3 - Protective orders (s.6/7/8/9/10).
Ch 4 - Breach s.33.
Ch 5 - DV-context criminal offences.
Ch 6 - DV homicide (Heffernan sustained provocation pathway).
Appendices - Defences; Evidence (s.16 CJA 2006 hostile witness, recanting); Risk Assessment + MultiAgency; Statutory Anchors; Case Law (ECtHR Opuz line: Opuz, Kontrova, Bevacqua, Branko Tomasic,
Talpis, A v Croatia, Osman); Back Matter.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 5 - Drugs
Front Matter - MDA architecture, drug schedules, mnemonics (SCAFFOLD, FIND, CHAIN,
PRESUMPTION).
Ethics - drug-investigation ethics, public health interface.
Ch 1 - MDA 1977 framework.
Ch 2 - Possession s.3.
Ch 3 - Possession for sale or supply s.15.
Ch 4 - s.15A value EUR 13,000+ presumptive sentencing.
Ch 5 - Production, cultivation, importation.
Ch 6 - Specialist offences (s.15B prisons, s.15C minors, NPS 2010 Act).
Ch 7 - Powers (s.23 search, s.24 arrest, s.26 warrant, Drug Trafficking Act 1996 7-day detention).
Appendices - Defences and Practice; Evidence (chain of custody, FSI, market value); CAB/POC; Statutory
Anchors; Case Law (s.15A sentencing line: Renald, Ducque, Long, Anderson); Back Matter.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 6 - Sexual Offences
Front Matter - victim-centred framework, forensic urgency, SATU, mnemonics (SUPPORT, FORENSIC,
DECLARE, CARE).
Ethics - six heightened Standards, six trip-wires, trauma on investigators.
Ch 1 - Sexual offences framework, 2017 Act consent doctrine.
Ch 2 - Rape (s.2 CL(R)A 1981 / s.4 CL(R)(A)A 1990).
Ch 3 - Sexual assault / aggravated sexual assault.
Ch 4 - Consent under 2017 Act in depth (s.48).
Ch 5 - Sexual offences against children (under-13, under-15, under-17, online grooming).
Ch 6 - Specialist (CSAM, trafficking, Coco's Law, FGM).
Ch 7 - Investigation framework (DARI/DSCB/GCCB/SOMU, SATU detail, historical CSA, Sex Offender
Register).
Appendices - Defences; Evidence (s.3 sexual history, recent complaint, special measures); Multi-Agency;
Statutory Anchors; Case Law (ECtHR positive obligations: X and Y v Netherlands, M.C. v Bulgaria, O'Keeffe
v Ireland landmark, Aydin); Back Matter.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 7 - Road Traffic
Front Matter - RTA architecture, Roads Policing structure, mnemonics (BREATH, CHECKPOINT,
CARELESS-DANGEROUS, SCENE).
Ethics - RTA-specific, six trip-wires.
Ch 1 - RTA Framework.
Ch 2 - Drink-driving s.4 RTA 2010.
Ch 3 - Drug-driving.
Ch 4 - Testing procedure (s.9-15 RTA 2010, Avadenei compliance).
Ch 5 - Dangerous and careless driving (s.51/52/53 RTA 1961, fatal collision).
Ch 6 - Other offences (no licence, no insurance, hit and run, mobile/seatbelt, scramblers).
Ch 7 - Powers (s.108 stop, MAT checkpoint, s.39 production, arrest, pursuit).
Appendices - Defences; Evidence (statutory form, calibration, MBRS); Fatal Collision Investigation; Statutory
Anchors; Case Law (Avadenei landmark, Finn, Spillane, McGarrigle, Cabot, Gillane); Back Matter.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 8 - Special Incidents
Front Matter - special incidents framework, mnemonics (DIGNIFIED, LOCATE, CALM, FLO).
Ethics - special incidents ethics, six trip-wires.
Ch 1 - Sudden and unexpected death (Coroner framework, suspicious indicators, death notification protocol).
Ch 2 - Missing persons (risk categorisation, AMBER Alert, sustained investigation).
Ch 3 - Mental health s.12 Mental Health Act 2001.
Ch 4 - Family liaison.
Ch 5 - Other special incidents (bomb threats, hostage, major emergencies, DVI).
Appendices - Practice; Evidence; Multi-Agency; Statutory Anchors; Case Law (Article 2 procedural obligation:
McCann, Jordan, Da Silva, Salman); Back Matter.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 9 - Cross-Cutting Reference
Front Matter - cross-cutting overview, mnemonics (ARMS, DATA, CUSTODY, COMMAND).
Ethics - cross-cutting ethics, five trip-wires.
Ch 1 - Weapons and firearms (Firearms Acts, NFOAP s.9 offensive weapons).
Ch 2 - Communications data framework.
Ch 3 - PULSE and PEMS.
Ch 4 - Command and control (Gold/Silver/Bronze).
Ch 5 - Specialist resources consolidated.
Ch 6 - Custody framework consolidated.
Ch 7 - Disclosure framework.
Appendices - Statutory Anchors; Case Law (CJEU communications data: Digital Rights Ireland, Tele2/Watson,
La Quadrature, Dwyer); Series Consolidation.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 10 - Criminal Intelligence
Front Matter - intelligence framework, what intelligence is vs evidence, old ways and new ways, mnemonics
(CYCLE, SOURCE, PLAN, PROTECT).
Ethics - intelligence ethics, seven trip-wires (grade inflation, fabrication, source compromise, covert overreach,
system abuse, improper-grounds targeting, intelligence-evidence confusion).
Ch 1 - The Intelligence Cycle and Intelligence-Led Policing (National Intelligence Model, tasking and
coordination).
Ch 2 - Grading and Evaluation (source / information / handling system; sanitisation).
Ch 3 - HUMINT - Human Intelligence (beat knowledge, community, witnesses, CHIS, source handling, agent
provocateur limit).
Ch 4 - OSINT - Open Source Intelligence (open-source boundary, tradecraft, private-data line).
Ch 5 - Technical and Surveillance Intelligence (CJ Surveillance Act 2009, physical surveillance, ANPR, CCTV,
comms data, device forensics).
Ch 6 - Financial Intelligence (STRs, FIU, lifestyle/proceeds, CAB, money laundering).
Ch 7 - Forensic and Database Intelligence (DNA database, AFIS, ballistics linkage, PULSE as intelligence tool,
the collator).
Ch 8 - Building, Analysing and Disseminating (corroboration, analytical products, the intelligence-evidence
divide and sterile corridor, threat-to-life, disruption).
Appendices - Practice and the Intelligence File; Evidence and Admissibility; Statutory Anchors; Case Law
(surveillance: Klass/Malone/Kennedy/Big Brother Watch; source protection: Ward/informer
privilege/Goodwin; Damache/Walsh/entrapment/JC); Back Matter.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

How to Use This Volume
This volume covers property crime investigation — the highest-volume category of offences a Mountjoy-based
or DMR North Central Garda will encounter. It is calibrated for the mid-level operational Garda who knows
the basics and needs operational depth for file-building, supervisor briefing, court preparation, and spotting
problems before they become defects.
The volume is organised in seven chapters by offence category, plus front and back matter:
• Chapter 1 — Theft (foundations + theft from person, shop, MPV)
• Chapter 2 — Burglary (dwelling, non-dwelling, aggravated)
• Chapter 3 — Robbery (simple, aggravated, attempted)
• Chapter 4 — Criminal Damage (simple, arson, threats)
• Chapter 5 — Handling and Possession of Stolen Property
• Chapter 6 — Unauthorised Taking of Motor Propelled Vehicle
• Chapter 7 — Deception and Fraud (operational level)
Each incident entry follows the same operational structure:
• Bold one-line definition + statutory citation
• Legal Framework — key Irish cases re-stated in full so the volume is self-contained
• European and Comparative Authority — ECtHR, CJEU, and persuasive UK / Commonwealth authority
• Cases That Went Before the Courts — worked examples with fact pattern, ruling, and lesson
• Track A — Live Incident (in progress or just occurred)
• Track B — Post-Fact Report (reported hours/days/weeks after the event)
• Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
• Interview Plan (PEACE-aligned, topic-by-topic)
• PULSE entry / PEMS exhibit handling / Investigation File Index
• Charge Selection — best charge + fallback charges + court of trial + maximum penalty
• Specimen Wording — caution, demand if any, notebook entry, statement opening, memo of interview
opener
• Defence Will Attack — typical vectors / appellate authority where defence won / how to bullet-proof
• Red Flag Escalation — when to call Detective Unit, NBCI, DVSAIU, GNDOCB, DOCB
• Common Pitfalls
• GDMM Decision Points
• Cross-references
Use the volume forwards (legal framework → operational application) when learning or preparing for court,
and backwards (need a practical answer → find the technique) when handling a live incident. Cross-references
between incidents are deliberate — a single situation often engages multiple offences (e.g. burglary may also
involve criminal damage, handling, and possession of stolen property).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The Two-Track Approach
Property crime is reported in two distinct ways. The investigation strategy for each is fundamentally different.
Conflating them is the most common reason files come back from the DPP with directions for further
investigation.

Track A — Live Incident
The incident is in progress, or has just occurred, when Gardaí arrive or are dispatched. The scene exists.
Witnesses are present. The suspect may still be there or recently departed. CCTV is fresh and recoverable.
Forensic windows are open.
Operational priorities: preserve life, preserve scene, preserve evidence, identify suspect, arrest where lawful,
secure witnesses.

Track B — Post-Fact Report
The incident was committed hours, days, or weeks ago. The injured party (IP) is reporting after the fact. The
scene is degraded or gone. Witnesses have moved on. CCTV may already have been overwritten. The suspect
is rarely on-scene. The forensic window may be closed.
Operational priorities: secure what remains, race the CCTV overwrite clock, document the IP's recollection
accurately, identify investigative leads (PULSE history, MO matches, similar offences), build the file from
secondary evidence.
KEY POINT — IDENTIFY THE TRACK IN THE FIRST FIVE MINUTES
If you treat a three-day-old burglary like a live one, you will arrive at a tidied scene, no exhibits, no CCTV
(overwritten or never retrieved), and a file the DPP will direct further investigation on.
If you treat a live incident like a post-fact one, you will fail to preserve evidence that was within your reach —
the worst possible outcome.
Use the TRACK check (Time of report, Recency of incident, Available evidence, CCTV window, Known
suspect) within the first five minutes. Each incident chapter sets out the priorities for both tracks.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Garda Decision-Making Model (GDMM)
The GDMM is the structured decision framework used throughout this volume. Every operational decision
point in every incident chapter is anchored to the GDMM. The model derives from the College of Policing
National Decision Model adapted for An Garda Síochána, and is consistent with the proportionality framework
recognised in Article 8 ECHR jurisprudence (S and Marper v UK; Gillan and Quinton v UK).

Article 8 ECHR check: every coercive act — arrest, search, seizure, detention — must be (a) in accordance
with the law, (b) for a legitimate aim, and (c) necessary and proportionate. Build that test into Stage 3 and 4. S
and Marper v UK (2008) and Gillan and Quinton v UK (2010) confirm Article 8 scrutiny applies to UK police
powers; the same proportionality framework applies in Ireland under both ECHR Act 2003 and Article 40.3 of
the Constitution.

European and Comparative Authority — Why It Matters
Irish criminal procedure is not insulated from European or comparative authority. ECHR rights are directly
enforceable in Irish courts via the ECHR Act 2003. CJEU authority binds where EU law is engaged (e.g. data
retention, EAW, victim's rights). UK and Commonwealth authority is persuasive on shared common-law
principles — the Supreme Court has cited UK Supreme Court, Australian High Court, and Canadian Supreme
Court authority on theft, robbery, dishonesty, and trespass elements.

Garda Code of Ethics — Applied to Property Crime
The Garda Síochána Code of Ethics (2017, revised 2022) is enacted under s.17 of the Garda Síochána Act 2005.
It is not aspirational — it is enforceable. Breach can found a disciplinary process under the Garda Síochána
(Discipline) Regulations 2007 and feed a suitability review. Every action in every chapter of this volume should
be testable against the Code. Throughout each incident chapter, an “Ethics Touchpoints” block flags the Codeengagement issues most likely to be probed by GSOC, by the courts, or by an internal review.

Code Engagement Points in Every Property Investigation
Each of the following recurs across the volume. Before any significant action, run the Code-check:
1. Is the power I am exercising lawful? (Std 2)
2. Am I treating the IP, suspect, witness with equal respect? (Std 4)
3. Am I handling information with care — PULSE accurate, not gossipy? (Std 3)
4. If a custody decision is involved — dignity, safety, medical, legal access? (Std 1)
5. Have I documented my decision-making? (Std 8, 9)
6. If I see a colleague stepping outside the Code, will I speak up? (Std 7)
THE TWO ETHICS TRAPS IN PROPERTY CRIME
Trap 1 — Volume fatigue. Thirty shop thefts a month leads to corner-cutting. The Code does not relax for volume.
The five-euro shop theft is investigated to the same standard as the five-thousand-euro burglary, even if the
prosecutorial outcome differs.
Trap 2 — “Known to us.” The fact a suspect is a repeat offender does not lower the evidence threshold. The
Code’s impartiality standard (Std 4) and the rule of law require that every charge be evidenced on its own facts,
not on reputation.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Compliment, Commend, Praise — When and How
The Code (Std 8) calls for personal responsibility — owning failures, and acknowledging good work. A wellhandled investigation often involves significant colleague contribution that deserves to be written up.
Throughout this volume, where a colleague’s contribution materially improves an outcome, a “Compliment
Point” flag is included. These are signals to consider:
• Written compliment to supervisor — informal note to Sergeant, copied to colleague, recording specific
contribution (e.g. “Garda X spotted the CCTV link that identified the suspect”).
• Formal commendation submission — where the contribution was exceptional (intervention preventing
serious harm, identification of organised series, etc.). Submission through Sergeant → Inspector →
Superintendent.
• Peer acknowledgment in case-conference — simple verbal recognition during file handover; reinforces
the culture and is small but real.
• Welfare-side recognition — where a colleague has dealt with a particularly difficult IP (distressed elderly
burglary victim, traumatised assault victim), flagging that in the report supports their record and welfare.
Why it matters: ethics is not only about catching wrongdoing. The Code asks for transparency and personal
responsibility — that includes naming good work in writing. A station culture where compliments are written
down is a station culture where the Code is lived, not framed on a wall.

Chapter 1 — Theft
Theft is the highest-volume property offence at Mountjoy and across the DMR North Central Division. The
legal framework appears simple — dishonest appropriation without consent and with intent to permanently
deprive — but every element is contested in court. Master the foundations, then apply them to each sub-incident.

1.0 Theft Foundations
Theft: a person is guilty of theft if they dishonestly appropriate property without the consent of its owner
and with the intention of depriving its owner of it — s.4(1) Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences)
Act 2001.

Legal Framework — Irish Authority
DPP v Foley [2007] 2 IR 486
Facts: Foley took a vehicle that had been left running by its owner. He drove it a short distance and abandoned
it. The defence argued there was no intention to permanently deprive — he had used it temporarily and left it
where it could be recovered.
Held: The Court held that intent to permanently deprive can be inferred from circumstances. Abandonment in
a location and condition that exposed the vehicle to loss or damage was sufficient to ground the inference. The
court need not find subjective desire to keep it forever.
Ratio: Intent to permanently deprive is inferred from objective circumstances, not from a defendant's stated
intention. Treating property as the accused's own to dispose of regardless of the owner's rights satisfies the test
(s.4(5)).
Operational application: When taking a statement from a suspect, capture every fact that bears on disposal
— where they left the property, what condition, whether they tried to return it. In the file, list every objective
circumstance that supports the inference.
DPP v Bowe [2017] IECA 215
Facts: Bowe entered a shop and crossed into the staff-only area behind the counter without consent. He
removed money from a till. The defence argued the shop was a public place and consent to enter was general.
Held: The Court of Appeal held that consent to enter a public premises does not extend to areas from which
the public is excluded. Crossing into a 'part of a building' (the staff-only area) without consent constituted entry
as a trespasser, sufficient to ground a burglary count alongside theft.
Ratio: Implied consent to enter premises does not extend to areas marked or understood as staff-only. Entry
into such a part is entry as a trespasser for burglary purposes (s.12), and the dishonest taking inside is theft.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Operational application: On any shop theft involving entry behind the counter or into a stockroom, consider
both s.4 theft and s.12 burglary. Photograph the layout. Get a manager's statement confirming the area was
staff-only.
The People (AG) v Thompson [1960] IR 70
Facts: Thompson moved goods within a shop with intent to steal them but was apprehended before leaving.
The question was whether the offence was complete.
Held: The Supreme Court held that asportation — the slightest movement of the property with intent —
completes the taking. The offence is complete inside the shop; the defendant need not leave with the property.
Ratio: Theft is complete on the slightest movement of property with dishonest intent. Detention need not await
the suspect leaving the premises.
Operational application: Store security and your statement should record the precise moment of appropriation
— where the goods were taken from, where the suspect moved them to, whether they were concealed. The
suspect's departure from the premises is corroborative but not necessary.
DPP v Brennan [1998] 4 IR 67
Facts: Brennan was found in possession of property that had been stolen recently. He could not give a
reasonable account of how he came to possess it.
Held: The Court applied the doctrine of recent possession: unexplained possession of recently stolen property
can ground an inference that the possessor either stole it or received it knowing it stolen. The inference is
rebuttable but the burden of providing a credible explanation is practical.
Ratio: Recent possession + absence of credible explanation = available inference of theft or handling. Time
gap between theft and possession is critical — the shorter the gap, the stronger the inference.
Operational application: Document the time gap between reported theft and discovery of suspect's possession.
Question the suspect on provenance under caution. Any answers — 'I bought it', 'I found it', 'someone gave it
to me' — must be verbatim and tested. Trace the alleged source.
DPP v O'Loughlin [1979] IR 85
Facts: O'Loughlin was found in possession of stolen property and charged with handling. The mens rea —
knowledge or belief that the goods were stolen — was contested.
Held: Mens rea for handling requires knowledge or belief that the goods were stolen, not mere suspicion. Wilful
blindness, however, will satisfy the test — a deliberate refusal to enquire when circumstances called for enquiry.
Ratio: Knowledge or belief that goods are stolen is required for handling; wilful blindness is included.
Suspicion alone is not enough.
Operational application: Ask about price paid, source, condition, packaging, paperwork. Disproportionately
low price, no receipt, packaging removed, serial numbers obliterated — each is an indicator of wilful blindness.

European and Comparative Authority
R v Ghosh [1982] QB 1053 (CA, England)
Test: The classic two-limb dishonesty test — (1) was the conduct dishonest by the ordinary standards of
reasonable and honest people; (2) did the defendant realise it would be regarded as dishonest by those standards.
Persuasive in Ireland and frequently cited.
Ivey v Genting Casinos [2017] UKSC 67
Test: The UK Supreme Court modified Ghosh — the subjective second limb is removed. Dishonesty is now
assessed objectively against ordinary standards, with reference only to what the defendant actually knew or
believed about the facts. Defence counsel in Ireland will cite both; know which the court is applying.
R v Hinks [2001] 2 AC 241 (HL)
Principle: Appropriation may exist even where property passed with consent or by gift. A defendant who
induced a vulnerable person to make 'gifts' was held to have appropriated the property dishonestly. Relevant to
theft-by-deception and elder-abuse cases.
R v Lawrence [1972] AC 626 (HL)

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Principle: Appropriation requires assumption of any of the rights of the owner. Foundational on the meaning
of 'appropriates' — still cited in Irish theft jurisprudence.

Cases That Went Before the Courts — Worked Examples
Worked Example 1 — The Hotel Lobby Bag-Snatch (anonymised)
Facts: Tourist in a city-centre hotel lobby placed her bag on the chair beside her while checking in. A male
suspect approached, grabbed the bag, and ran. Hotel CCTV captured the entire incident from three angles.
Suspect was apprehended on Talbot Street five minutes later by a foot patrol who had heard the description
over Tetra.
What went right: (1) Reception called 999 immediately with a clear description. (2) Foot patrol moved to the
predicted egress route. (3) Hotel CCTV was preserved on-scene by the investigating Garda with a written
request and signature in the hotel's own register. (4) Notebook entries by both arresting and CCTV-seizing
Garda matched within minutes.
What went wrong: Bag had been touched by reception staff trying to assist before Gardaí arrived. Forensic
recovery of the bag was contaminated. Some valuables had already been removed by the suspect and were
never recovered.
Outcome: Guilty plea to theft contrary to s.4 — the CCTV evidence and identification were overwhelming.
Sentence: 18 months suspended with conditions. The forensic contamination did not matter because the case
did not depend on it.
Lesson: When the case rests on CCTV and ID, forensic loss is survivable. When it rests on forensic recovery,
contamination is fatal. Identify which evidence type your case rests on within the first 15 minutes.
Worked Example 2 — The Supermarket Shoplifting Run (anonymised)
Facts: Repeat suspect entered a Dublin city-centre supermarket. CCTV showed her selecting six bottles of
premium spirits, concealing them in a large coat, and leaving without paying. Detained at the door by store
security. Suspect provided false name and address.
What went right: Security held her at the door until Gardaí arrived. CCTV was preserved by the store's loss
prevention manager and burned to disc in the Garda's presence. A receipt-of-evidence form was signed.
What went wrong: Arresting Garda accepted the suspect's stated name and address without verification.
PULSE incident raised against the wrong identity. Bench warrant later issued against an innocent person whose
ID had been used. Months of work to unwind.
Outcome: Original case proceeded but the identity error generated a separate GSOC complaint from the
innocent party. Lesson learned at considerable cost.
Lesson: Verify identity — PULSE photographic lookup, fingerprint check at the station, or holding identity
through arrest. Do not rely on what a suspect tells you.
Worked Example 3 — Theft from MPV (anonymised)
Facts: Owner parked their car overnight on a residential side street. Returned the following morning to find the
passenger window smashed and a laptop bag taken. No CCTV in the immediate area. No witnesses. Reported
the next morning.
What went right: Investigating Garda walked the street and identified two domestic CCTV systems with
potentially relevant coverage. Within 48 hours, statements and footage had been requested by formal letter and
one yielded footage of a male suspect with a similar build approaching the car.
What went wrong: The CCTV request to a small business across the street was made on day six. The system
overwrote on a 5-day cycle. The footage was already gone.
Outcome: No prosecution. The case file was closed for want of identification evidence.
Lesson: The CCTV preservation clock starts the moment the offence occurred, not the moment you arrive. On
any Track B incident, get every CCTV request out within 24 hours of report — written, hand-delivered if
necessary, with a copy retained in the file.

1.1 Theft from Person
Theft from the person: theft where the property is taken from the body or immediate presence of the
injured party (mugging, bag-snatch, pickpocketing, distraction theft) — contrary to s.4 Criminal Justice
(Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001.
Distinction from robbery: if force is used on the person or the threat of force is used immediately before or at
the time of the taking, the offence is robbery (s.14), not theft. The line can be fine — a pickpocket who is
detected and pushes the victim away to escape may cross into robbery (R v Hale, appropriation as continuing
act).

Legal Framework
All elements of theft (1.0 above) apply. Additionally:
• Use of force converts the offence to robbery (s.14) — see Chapter 3.
• Where the IP is targeted because of vulnerability (intoxicated, elderly, asleep), this is an aggravating factor
for sentence.
• Theft from person frequently engages identification evidence — ADVOKATE assessment required.
• Where the IP is a tourist, statement should be taken urgently with witness contact details, given the risk of
departure from the jurisdiction.

European and Comparative Authority
Salduz v Turkey (2008) 49 EHRR 19
Operational impact: On arrest of any suspect for theft from person, solicitor consultation must precede
interview. No exceptions on operational urgency grounds without recorded Beuze-compatible justification.
R v Lambie [1982] AC 449 (HL)
Principle: Theft can be committed even where the property comes into the suspect's hands lawfully if there is
later dishonest appropriation. Relevant in distraction-theft cases where a suspect borrows a phone 'to make a
call' and then runs.
R v Pitham and Hehl (1977) 65 Cr App R 45
Principle: Offering to sell another person's property to a third party (without the owner's consent) is
appropriation, even before any physical movement. Useful where pickpocket attempts to dispose to a
confederate.

Cases That Went Before the Courts — Worked Examples
Worked Example — Talbot Street Distraction Theft
Facts: Elderly IP was approached on Talbot Street by a male who asked for directions while a second suspect
removed her purse from her open handbag. Both suspects fled in different directions. IP made a description
statement at the scene to the responding unit. CCTV from a nearby retailer captured both suspects on approach
and departure.
What went right: Unit took the description over Tetra before transporting the IP. Two further units intercepted
both suspects within 15 minutes on Marlborough Street and Sean McDermott Street respectively. Stop-andsearch recovered the purse. Custody and arrest grounds were noted contemporaneously by both arresting
members.
What went wrong: Identification parade was not held. Defence at trial argued that dock identification (the IP
pointing to the defendants in court) was insufficient. The Casey warning was given but the defence appealed
on the strength of the identification.
Outcome: Conviction upheld on appeal because the CCTV, the recovered property in the suspects' possession,
and the corroborative description provided a sufficient evidential basis independent of dock ID. But the case is
a warning.
Lesson: Where reasonably practicable, arrange a formal identification parade or VIPER procedure. Where
CCTV is overwhelming, dock ID with Casey warning may suffice, but never assume.

Track A — Live Incident (In Progress or Just Occurred)
Suspect may still be on-scene or in flight. Priorities in order:
7. Locate and care for the IP. Are they injured? Call ambulance if needed. Reassure.
8. Capture description over Tetra immediately — height, build, clothing (head down), direction of flight,
distinguishing features. Other units intercept.
9. Secure the scene of taking — short cordon. Place of taking, place where IP was standing.
10. Identify witnesses present. Names, numbers, in-the-moment account.
11. Identify CCTV — store fronts, ATMs, taxis with dash-cams, residential systems. Note the time precisely;
CCTV recovery depends on it.
12. Take an initial verbal account from the IP. Do not yet take a full statement — reassure, get the basics,
escort to station or remain at scene as IP prefers.
13. If a suspect is detained nearby by another unit, attend the stop. Confirm identification by IP if proximity
allows (taking care with Turnbull/Casey principles).
14. Photograph the scene and the IP’s standing position before bystanders move.
15. Notebook entry: time of arrival, IP first words, description given, units alerted, CCTV identified.
16. Update PULSE incident category 'Theft from person'.

Track B — Post-Fact Report
IP reports hours, days, or weeks after the event. Priorities in order:
17. Take a full statement immediately. Capture the IP's recollection while it is freshest. Do not delay to await
convenient time.
18. Establish the time and place precisely. This is the anchor for CCTV recovery.
19. Description of suspect(s) — height, build, clothing, ethnicity, distinguishing features, accent.
20. Items taken — detailed list with serial numbers, IMEI for phones, distinctive features for jewellery.
21. Race the CCTV clock. Within 24 hours of report, identify every camera with potential coverage of the
location at the time. Issue written CCTV requests with the time-window specified. Hand-deliver where
possible and retain copies.
22. Apply for s.10 CJA 2006 search warrant if any CCTV holder declines to cooperate. The warrant is granted
by a District Judge on sworn information.
23. Bank / phone trace: if a bank card or phone was taken, contact the issuer for transaction records or pings.
This requires a s.7 CJA 2011 disclosure request (financial) or an arrangement with the network.
24. PULSE history check at the location. Is this a repeat hotspot? Are there MO matches with named suspects
or PULSE persons of interest?
25. Witness canvass at the location at the same day/time of week, in case regulars were present and recall the
event.
26. Photograph the location for the file as it now is, with reference to where the IP was.

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Power of arrest:
• s.4 Criminal Law Act 1997 — arrestable offence (theft is punishable by 5 years or more on indictment;
s.4 applies).
• Without warrant where reasonable suspicion is held by the arresting member personally (DPP v Walsh).
• On arrest, state the offence in plain terms (Christie v Leachinsky / DPP v Pringle), administer caution,
identify yourself, and convey to a Garda station.
Where the suspect is detained at scene by store security or by a bystander, the detention is lawful only if
reasonable; immediate Garda attendance is essential. Document the time of detention and the time of arrest
separately. A prolonged civilian detention can ground a defence challenge under Article 5 ECHR.

Defence Will Attack
Typical attack vectors
• Identification: the IP only saw the suspect briefly, in poor light, from behind, under stress. ADVOKATE
failures.
• Continuity of CCTV: who handled the disc between premises and PEMS, was the operator's statement
obtained, are the times on the disc accurate.
• Caution and arrest grounds: DPP v Walsh — did the arresting member personally hold suspicion.
• Solicitor access: was any incriminating question put before solicitor consultation — DPP v Gormley &
White, Salduz, Beuze.
• Recent possession: the explanation offered for possession of the IP’s property was reasonable and the
prosecution has not negatived it (DPP v Brennan).
• Intoxication of IP: the IP’s recollection is unreliable. Statement was not contemporaneous.
Appellate authority where defence succeeded
Examples where convictions for theft and related offences have been overturned or unsafe verdicts directed:
• DPP v O'Reilly [1990] 2 IR 415 — inadequate Casey warning; identification evidence was insufficient
without corroboration.
• DPP v Gormley & White [2014] IESC 17 — questioning before solicitor consultation rendered inculpatory
statement inadmissible.
• DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC 1 — denial of solicitor presence during interview engaged Article 6; conviction
quashed.
• Salduz v Turkey (2008) — ECtHR foundational; the same principles applied in DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31
retrospectively engage Article 6 protection.
How to bullet-proof
34. ADVOKATE every identification statement. Build the eight elements into your s.21 CEA 1992 IP
statement.
35. Take a formal s.21 statement from every CCTV operator confirming the disc, time, format, and chain of
custody.
36. Notebook entries by every member in the chain. Times match. Grounds personally articulated.
37. Solicitor consultation logged before any substantive interview. Interview recorded. Caution administered
at every restart.
38. Recent possession: question the suspect specifically on provenance under caution; record verbatim. Trace
the alleged source independently.
39. Where IP intoxicated, take a brief preserved account at scene and a full statement when sober; explain the
gap in your file cover report.
40. Photograph the scene contemporaneously — the suspect's flight path, the IP's position, lighting conditions.

Red Flag Escalation
• Suspect uses or threatens a weapon — this is robbery (s.14), call Detective Unit immediately.
• IP is seriously injured — ambulance, scene cordon, call Detective Unit; consider s.3 / s.4 NFOAPA charges
in parallel.
• Multiple suspects acting in concert — organised theft team — consider GNDOCB engagement.
• Suspect is a juvenile — Diversion Programme consideration mandatory (s.18 Children Act 2001); see Vol
9.
• IP is vulnerable (elderly, disabled, child) — victim support liaison and prioritise the file.
• Cross-border or non-Irish national suspect — INTERPOL flag, immigration check via GNIB.

Common Pitfalls
• Accepting suspect's stated identity without verification — see Worked Example 2 above.
• Failing to administer caution before any substantive question at scene (DPP v Pringle / DPP v Conroy).
• Allowing prolonged civilian detention by store security before Garda arrival without documenting times.
• Letting CCTV preservation slide past day 1. Treat day 0 as the trigger.
• Conducting dock identification only — no parade, no VIPER — in a case where the IP's view was brief.
• Failing to identify other potential offences: assault, robbery, criminal damage during the same incident.
• Not photographing the scene before bystanders move — once disturbed it cannot be reconstructed.

1.2 Theft from Shop (Shoplifting)
Theft from shop: dishonest appropriation of goods from a retail premises without consent of the owner
and with intention to permanently deprive — s.4 Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001.
Note: where entry crosses into a staff-only area, also consider burglary contrary to s.12 (DPP v Bowe; R
v Walkington).

Legal Framework
All elements of theft apply (1.0). Specific operational considerations:
• Asportation — the slightest movement suffices (Thompson). The offence is complete inside the shop.
• Concealment is strong evidence of dishonest intent. Document it precisely.
• 'Pre-meditation' or 'shopping with intent' (booster bags, foil-lined coats) is an aggravating factor for
sentence and may ground a 'going equipped' charge under s.15.
• Where security detains the suspect at the door, the detention is a citizen's arrest under s.4 Criminal Law Act
1997 (anyone may arrest a person committing or who has committed an arrestable offence). Document the
times.

European and Comparative Authority
R v Morris [1984] AC 320 (HL)
Principle: Label-swapping in a shop is appropriation — assumption of the right of the owner to determine
price. Useful where suspect alters or swaps labels before going through the till.
R v Davies [1988] Crim LR 762
Principle: Concealment of an item in clothing or a bag while still in the shop is sufficient appropriation. The
defendant need not leave the store.

Cases That Went Before the Courts — Worked Examples
Worked Example — The Self-Checkout Underscan
Facts: Suspect used a self-checkout. CCTV showed her scanning a few items and placing other items —
unscanned — into her bag at the bagging area. She paid for the scanned items and left. Loss prevention
apprehended her outside.
What went right: Loss prevention had CCTV from two angles — above the self-checkout and at the exit.
Items recovered matched what was unscanned. Receipts for what she did pay for were retained.
What went wrong: Investigating Garda did not get a statement from the self-checkout till audit — the till's
own digital record of what was scanned and at what time. At court, defence argued the CCTV could not reliably
show which items were and were not scanned.
Outcome: Acquittal. The court was not satisfied beyond reasonable doubt as to which items were unscanned.
Lesson: On any self-checkout theft, get the till audit / receipt printout as well as CCTV. The digital record is
independent corroboration.
Worked Example — The Booster-Bag Operation
Facts: Organised group entered a city-centre pharmacy chain in rotation over a week, using foil-lined 'booster
bags' to defeat security tags. Stock loss reported as 4,500 EUR. Loss prevention obtained CCTV across the
week showing the same individuals repeatedly.
What went right: Loss prevention compiled a portfolio of CCTV across the week with timestamps and item
lists. PULSE check showed the suspects had similar marks in other DMR North Central premises. Joint
operation with the Garda Crime Prevention Unit identified the group as part of an organised retail crime team.
What went wrong: When two of the group were arrested, the searching members did not seize the booster
bags or check for false linings. The physical proof of the modus operandi was lost.
Outcome: Guilty pleas to theft on the strength of CCTV, but the s.15 'going equipped' or s.71B Criminal Justice
Act 2006 organised-crime aggravation could not be pursued.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Lesson: On any organised retail crime, search at arrest for the tools of the trade — booster bags, magnetic
detachers, fake receipts. They are critical to the s.15 / organised-crime case.

Track A — Live Incident
Almost all shop thefts come to Gardaí as Track A — store security has detained the suspect.
41. On arrival, identify yourself, identify suspect, identify detaining staff.
42. Brief account from loss prevention — what was seen, how detained, where the goods are now.
43. Examine the recovered goods. Photograph in situ before they are moved.
44. View the CCTV with the loss prevention manager. Confirm time, angles, what is shown.
45. Decide arrest — the evidence threshold is normally easily met. State the offence in plain terms, caution,
convey to station.
46. Take a s.21 statement from loss prevention (or arrange one at the station / by appointment).
47. Seize: CCTV (or arrange transfer); recovered goods; suspect's bag / coat / booster equipment.
48. Notebook: arrival, suspect first words, evidence noted, exhibits seized.
49. Pre-arrest, ensure the IP company can provide a representative for a s.21 statement (e.g. store manager
confirming ownership and value).

Track B — Post-Fact Report
Less common but happens — stock loss noticed later, suspect on CCTV but not detained, or organised retail
crime sweeps reviewed afterward.
50. Confirm the report — stock count, audit, CCTV review by loss prevention.

• Theft is arrestable under s.4 Criminal Law Act 1997 (theft is punishable by 5 years or more on indictment).
• Personal reasonable suspicion required (Walsh).
• Caution at arrest; solicitor consultation at station before interview (Salduz / Gormley).
• Where the same suspect has a history of shop theft, consider summons (CM13) for first or minor incidents
and full arrest for aggravated or repeat. Document the rationale.

Typical attack vectors

1.3 Theft from Motor Propelled Vehicle (TFMPV)
Theft from MPV: dishonest appropriation of property from inside, on, or attached to a motor propelled
vehicle, without the consent of the owner and with intention to permanently deprive — s.4 Criminal
Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001. Where the vehicle is broken or interfered with to gain
access, also consider criminal damage (s.2 CDA 1991) and possession of tools to enter (s.15 CJ(T&FO)A
2001).

Legal Framework
• Theft elements as 1.0 apply.
• Forced entry to the vehicle is criminal damage; charge in addition where appropriate.
• Possession of tools (jiggler keys, slim jims, signal relay devices) is s.15 CJ(T&FO)A 2001 — 'going
equipped'.
• Where the vehicle itself is taken with the property inside, consider s.112 RTA 1961 UTMV — see Chapter
6.
• Where catalytic converters are taken: theft is s.4; the offence is treated as theft of a vehicle part, valuation
per FSI / market.

European and Comparative Authority
R v Easom [1971] 2 QB 315 (CA)
Principle: Conditional intent — 'I will steal whatever I find inside that is worth taking' — is sufficient mens
rea. Useful in 'car-trying' cases where the suspect was caught checking handles along a street.
R v Re-A [1986] (Hong Kong CFA)
Principle: Persuasive on similar-fact evidence: repeated MO across multiple vehicles can be admitted to prove
identity. Relevant in serial-TFMPV files.

Cases That Went Before the Courts — Worked Examples
Worked Example — The Apartment-Carpark Series
Facts: A series of TFMPVs reported across an underground carpark over a fortnight. Window smashes; laptops,
satnavs, gym bags taken. CCTV available in the carpark but low quality. Each incident reported next morning
by the IP.
What went right: PULSE incidents linked under a series tag. The investigating Garda walked the carpark with
the building manager and identified four high-quality cameras on adjacent buildings that had not been
considered. Footage was preserved within 48 hours of being identified.
What went wrong: Glass fragments were not preserved from any of the smashed windows. At trial against a
known suspect found in possession of similar property, defence successfully argued that no forensic link tied
the suspect to the breakings.
Outcome: Convictions on possession of stolen property (s.18) but acquittal on the substantive theft and criminal
damage. Glass-fragment recovery would have changed that.
Lesson: On any forced-entry TFMPV, preserve glass fragments. They are a forensic gold mine — microscopic
fragments adhere to clothing for days and tie a suspect to a specific breaking.
Worked Example — The Signal-Relay Car Theft
Facts: High-end vehicle taken from a driveway overnight. No forced entry. CCTV from neighbour showed two
suspects with an electronic device approaching the front door, pausing, then walking to the car which then
unlocked.
What went right: Investigating Garda recognised the MO — signal-relay attack on keyless entry. Statement
from the IP confirmed the keys had been on a hall table within the radius the relay would amplify. PULSE intel
circulated; a similar MO had been linked to a named team.
What went wrong: The IP had not yet activated the vehicle's tracker subscription. By the time the team was
identified the car was likely abroad. No recovery.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Outcome: Intel file built; no immediate prosecution but the named team were targeted in a later operation.
Lesson: Modern car theft MO often leaves no forced-entry signs. Educate IPs at the scene about Faraday
pouches and the importance of activating tracker subscriptions. Build PULSE intel even where no immediate
prosecution lies.

Track A — Live Incident
Rare in TFMPV — most are Track B — but live calls do happen (suspect interrupted by IP returning, or
witnessed by neighbour).
69. On arrival, identify the suspect's location and direction of flight if departed. Circulate description.
70. Identify the IP and the vehicle. Photograph any forced entry in situ.
71. Preserve glass fragments — evidence bag, sealed, signed, exhibit number.
72. Preserve any items dropped or left by the suspect (tools, gloves, clothing). Forensic gold.
73. Scene cordon around the vehicle until forensic examination can be arranged where appropriate.
74. Witness canvass — neighbours, passers-by, any vehicles dash-cammed in the area.
75. CCTV identification as 1.1.

Track B — Post-Fact Report
76. Statement from IP — last time vehicle seen secure, location, condition, contents, value.
77. Photograph the damage — window, lock, door.
78. Preserve glass from the scene if present. Bag and exhibit. Even days later glass can be matched to clothing
of a suspect arrested in possession of similar property.
79. Race the CCTV clock. Identify every camera with line of sight on the vehicle's parking location.
80. Insurer / tracker query — some modern vehicles have telematics that record open/close events with
timestamps.
81. PULSE history check at the location and surrounding streets. Series tag if multiple.
82. Bank / phone trace for items taken (laptops, phones — IMEI / serial circulated to second-hand and pawn
industry via Garda Crime Prevention).

• Theft is arrestable: s.4 CLA 1997.
• If found in possession of stolen goods: s.18 CJ(T&FO)A 2001 (possession of stolen property).
• If found with tools (jigglers, signal relay, slim jim): s.15 CJ(T&FO)A 2001 (going equipped) — arrestable,
5 yrs.
• Search incident to arrest under s.6 CJA 1984 — search the person and immediate vicinity for evidence.

Interview Plan

83. Account of movements during the relevant time window.
84. Knowledge of the IP, the vehicle, the location.
85. If found in possession of similar property — provenance, where bought, from whom, what price.
86. If found with tools — what for, where obtained.
87. Inference warnings under s.18, s.19 CJA 1984 as applicable — mark on suspect from glass fragments,
item in possession, etc.

Chapter 2 — Burglary
Burglary is one of the most serious property offences — maximum penalty 14 years on indictment for the basic
offence (s.12), life imprisonment for the aggravated form (s.13). Burglary dwelling is uniquely intrusive: the
IP’s sense of security in their own home is breached. Investigative rigour must match. The chapter covers the
three statutory variants and the operational realities of each.

2.0 Burglary — Foundations and Legal Framework
BURGLARY: A person who enters any building or part of a building as a trespasser, and with the intent
to commit an arrestable offence, is guilty of burglary. Contrary to s.12(1) Criminal Justice (Theft and
Fraud Offences) Act 2001.

The Four Elements
93. Enters — includes any insertion of part of the body or an instrument; entry need not be of the whole person.
94. Building or part of a building — broadly interpreted (see MacEoin and Quilligan below).
95. As a trespasser — without permission, or in excess of permission given.
96. With intent to commit an arrestable offence — typically theft, but also assault, criminal damage, sexual
offence, etc.
S.12(2): a person is also guilty of burglary if, having entered any building or part of a building as a trespasser,
they commit or attempt an arrestable offence inside (“ultimate intent” variant).

Key Authority — Re-stated in Full
DPP v Quilligan [1986] IR 495 — The Trespass Element
Facts: Defendant entered a premises and the trespass element was at issue.
Holding: Trespass requires entry without permission, or entry in excess of any permission given. Lawful entry
into one part of premises does not extend to all parts. Permission to be on the shop floor does not extend to
staff-only areas.
Why It Matters: Critical for retail and hospitality burglary cases. The barred customer who re-enters and steals
is in burglary territory, not just theft. Document the access points and signage; statement from manager on
access scope.
DPP v MacEoin [1978] IR 27 — Building / Part of Building
Holding: “Building” construed broadly; “part of a building” engages where a person is lawfully in one part but
trespasses into another.
Why It Matters: Hotels (guest in lobby, trespasser in another guest’s room), residential buildings with common
parts, mixed-use blocks — all engage “part of building” analysis.
DPP v Brennan [1998] 4 IR 67 — Recent Possession (revisited)
Already summarised in Chapter 1 and in the Case Law Appendix. Critical to burglary: suspect found with
property stolen from a recent burglary, no satisfactory explanation — inference is robust.
ECHR Article 8 — Right to Respect for Home
Burglary is the criminal-law mirror of Article 8 protection. Search warrants must be appropriately grounded
and proportionate (see Damache, Niemietz). Where Gardaí conduct a search at the suspect’s home, you operate
under the same constitutional framework that protects the burglary IP.

Ethics Touchpoints (Foundations)
• Std 4 (Respect/Equality): a burglary in a marginalised area receives the same investigative depth as one in
an affluent area. Same forensic call-out, same CCTV pursuit, same supervisor brief.
• Std 1 (Persons in Custody): suspects detained for burglary are often vulnerable (addiction, homelessness).
Dignity in custody is not optional.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• Std 3 (Information): IP identity, address, and contents inventory are sensitive. PULSE narrative precise,
not gossipy.

2.1 Burglary Dwelling
BURGLARY DWELLING: Entry of a building or part of a building used as a dwelling, as a trespasser,
with intent to commit an arrestable offence (or, having so entered, committing such an offence). Contrary
to s.12(1)(a) and s.12(3) CJ(T&FO)A 2001 — “dwelling” aggravates sentencing (presumptive minimums
under the Criminal Justice Act 2007 in some circumstances).
The most invasive of property offences. IP impact is significant beyond financial loss — sleep disturbance,
anxiety, reluctance to leave home. Investigative posture should reflect this. In DMR North Central, burglary
dwelling tends to cluster in two profiles: (a) high-risk-period offences (Christmas, holiday absences, daylight
burglaries in workday hours) targeting unoccupied properties, and (b) opportunistic entries through unsecured
windows / doors. Both require different evidence strategies.

Track A — Live Incident (Burglary in Progress / Just Occurred)
First 60 Minutes
97. Approach: the most important moment of the shift. Suspect may still be inside. Approach cautiously,
position units to cover egress, consider Armed Support Unit (ASU) call-out if firearms or violence
indication.
98. Inhabitants: account for all occupants. Children, elderly, vulnerable. Medical assistance if injured.
Reassurance — they have just been violated in their own home.
99. Suspect on scene: arrest under s.4 CJA 1984 (burglary is arrestable). PROOF check. If suspect flees, pursue
cautiously; circulate description; brief incoming units.
100. Scene preservation: do not allow occupants to clean up. Establish cordon. Common Approach Path.
ELOG (Entry Log) of every person crossing.
101. Tool / instrument identification — photograph and exhibit any tools (jemmy, screwdriver, glove,
balaclava) at point of recovery.
102. Notebook: full account of arrival, observations, occupant first words, scene state, recovered articles.
103. Brief Detective Unit / Scenes of Crime as appropriate — ALL burglary dwellings warrant forensic
consideration. The decision not to call SOCO is the decision worth justifying, not the decision to call them.
COMPLIMENT POINT — RECOGNISE THE FIRST RESPONDER
The first Garda on a live burglary dwelling who keeps occupants calm, sets a proper cordon, preserves forensic
potential, and circulates a useful suspect description has done foundational investigative work. If the file leads to
a charge, this work usually does not feature in the cover report unless someone names it.
Action: in your investigating Garda’s statement, name the first responders’ contribution by reference. If
exceptional (intervention preventing harm, fast suspect identification), submit a written compliment to the
Sergeant for inclusion in the colleague’s record.

Scene Management
Burglary dwelling scenes warrant full Locard treatment — every contact leaves a trace. Forensic potential:
fingerprints (door frames, glass surfaces, drawer fronts, jewellery boxes), DNA (forced entry blood, discarded
gloves, drink containers, cigarette butts), tool marks (forced doors, broken locks), footprints, fibres.
• Cordon: outer cordon to manage public/media, inner cordon at the building boundary, sterile zone within.
• Common Approach Path (CAP): single defined route in and out; this becomes the only path used;
everything else is preserved.
• ELOG: every person who crosses the cordon, time in / time out, role. Includes occupants, Gardaí, SOCO,
paramedics, locksmith.
• Photographic record: before SOCO start, photograph the entire scene as found. Wide angle, mid-range,
close-up. Establish the as-found state.
• Triage: identify the most evidentially productive areas. Point of entry, target areas (bedrooms typically),
suspect path if known.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Evidence Targets
• Forensic: SOCO call-out for dustings, swabs, photography. ETD (eternal triangle of contamination —
preserve, recover, document).
• CCTV: own-premises (where present), neighbour cameras, public realm, business cameras on
approach/egress routes, transport hubs.
• Tools and discarded items: jemmies, screwdrivers, gloves, balaclavas, swag bags, discarded paperwork.
• IP statement: property schedule (full descriptions, serials, photographs of similar items where uniques
unavailable), insurance position, what last known position of items, who has access to the property.
• Inhabitant accounts: separately, what they heard, when, what they did.
• Neighbour canvass: door-to-door, both sides plus opposite plus diagonals. Have they CCTV? Did they
see/hear anything? Have any unusual persons been observed in recent days?
• Suspect property (if arrested): full search; cash and items matching IP’s schedule; phones (digital evidence
chapter applies); tools; outerwear for forensic transfer.
Witness Strategy
• IP / Inhabitants: primary statements. Allow for shock — supplementary statement after 24–48 hours when
memory consolidates. Property schedule may be added to over subsequent days.
• Neighbours / Witnesses: canvass within 24 hours. ADVOKATE any witness who reports seeing a person
of interest.
• Owner of recovered property: if items recovered separately (pawnshop, second sale), the chain witness.

Track B — Post-Fact Report
The IP returns home (from holiday, workday, weekend) and finds the house entered. Time elapsed from incident
may be minutes to weeks. Scene is contaminated by IP’s movement before reporting. CCTV window may be
closing. Forensic recovery is reduced but not eliminated.
First Actions on Report
104. Sit IP down. Get the bracket: when last known secure / when discovered. Establish the time window
precisely.
105. Discuss what they have touched / moved since discovery. Note this carefully — contamination is now
part of the file.
106. Walk the scene with the IP for an initial property inventory. Photograph each disturbed area.
107. Decide on forensic call-out: even Track B burglary dwellings often warrant SOCO if the bracket is not
too wide and obvious points of entry exist.
108. CCTV preservation — the IP’s bracket is your CCTV window. Public realm, retail, neighbour cameras,
transport. Within shift.
109. Neighbour canvass — doors knocked even at 7 days. People remember unusual activity. CCTV on
private homes is increasing.
110. PULSE incident with full property schedule.
Evidence Recovery in Cold Conditions
• Forensic potential drops but is not zero. Identified points of entry (forced doors, broken windows) can still
yield results 72 hours+ later.
• MO matching: burglary dwellings often cluster by MO (entry method, target items, area). Brief Detective
Unit; cluster across district.
• Pawnshop / second-hand checks for high-value items: jewellery, watches, electronics, branded goods.
• ANPR for the bracket window if a vehicle of interest emerges.

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Burglary is arrestable (max 14 years). S.4 CJA 1984 detention available. The 12-hour detention clock
(extensible by Superintendent under s.4(3) to 24 hours total) gives operational time for interview,
fingerprinting, ID, search of address.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Specimen arrest words: “I am Garda [name] from Mountjoy. I am arresting you under section 4 of the
Criminal Justice Act 1984 on suspicion of burglary contrary to section 12 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and
Fraud Offences) Act 2001, said to have occurred on [date] at [address]. You are not obliged to say anything
unless you wish to do so, but anything you say will be taken down in writing and may be given in evidence.”
Search Powers on Arrest
• S.6 CJA 1984 — search of arrested person at place of arrest / station.
• S.10 CJA 2006 — search warrant for premises in connection with arrestable offence (District Judge
application).
• S.48 Larceny Act 1916 (still operative as amended) — search for stolen property.

Interview Plan (PEACE-aligned)
Burglary dwelling interviews are long, structured, and topic-disciplined. Plan the topics, do not let them blur.
111. Account: open invitation to suspect to give their version of where they were on [date/time bracket].
Listen, do not interrupt, take full notes.
112. Movements / Alibi: probe each piece of the account — named places, named persons, time markers.
113. CCTV / forensic links: present each piece of evidence properly cautioned. “We have a fingerprint on
the door frame at [address]. How do you account for that?”
114. Recovered property: each item separately. Inference questions (s.18 / s.19 CJA 1984) where statutory
criteria met.
115. Modus operandi: probe knowledge of the area, the IP, the property — do not disclose intel beyond what
evidence requires.
116. Closure: summary of account, opportunity to add anything, end-time recorded.
ETHICS TOUCHPOINT — INTERVIEW OF VULNERABLE SUSPECTS
Many burglary suspects are addicts. Withdrawal, intoxication, mental health all engage Std 1 (custody) and Std
4 (respect / equality).
Practical: medical assessment on arrival; appropriate adult if intellectual disability; pause interview if suspect
distressed; offer breaks; record everything.
Salduz / Doyle / Gormley: lawyer access is constitutional and Convention-protected. Do not interview before
requested consultation has happened.

PULSE / PEMS / File Index
PULSE category: Burglary Dwelling.
PULSE narrative: Bracket (last secure / discovered), location precision, points of entry, property schedule
(full), forensic deployment, suspect description if any, MO.
PEMS: Every recovered item separately exhibited; SOCO submissions tracked; CCTV per source on separate
labelled media.

Defence Will Attack
Typical Attack Vectors

118. Continuity of forensic chain — from scene to lab to court. Defence will probe every signature on the
exhibit label.
119. Search warrant validity — Damache-style challenge; was the warrant properly grounded; was it issued
by an authority independent of the investigation.
120. Trespass element — “my client thought the door was open, thought he was permitted, was looking for
[name].”
121. Mens rea at point of entry — the intent to commit an arrestable offence must exist at the moment of
entry (s.12(1)(a)), not formed later. Defence will argue the suspect entered with no plan.
122. Specific property — the IP’s schedule may be challenged item-by-item; provenance disputes.
Appellate Authority Where Defence Won
• DPP v Cunningham [2012] IECCA 64 — evidence excluded where warrant defects amounted to
constitutional breach (pre-JC, but instructive).
• DPP v Damache [2012] IESC 11 — s.29 OASA warrant struck down; evidence from that route
inadmissible.
• DPP v O’Reilly [2009] IECCA 18 — conviction quashed where CCTV identification was sole basis and
image quality poor.
• Salduz v Turkey [GC] 2008 — admissions excluded where suspect had no lawyer access during
interrogation.
• DPP v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17 — admissions excluded where made before requested solicitor
consultation.
How to Bullet-Proof
• Forensic chain: every transfer signed, every exhibit logged in PEMS, every step photographed where
possible.
• Warrants: through District Judge, properly grounded information laid on oath, copy retained, statement of
execution.
• CCTV: full footage including approach and egress, not just key moments. Statement of operator (s.30 CEA
1992). Time-sync documented.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• Suspect interviews: lawyer access provided and documented; cautions clear; inference warnings precise.
• Trespass: statement from owner / occupier expressly excluding the suspect’s permission to enter (including
any prior permission that was revoked).
• Intent at entry: build inference — reconnaissance, choice of timing, equipment carried, target selection —
all support pre-entry intent.

Red Flag Escalation
• Series of burglary dwellings in district / division — brief Detective Unit, consider cross-district analyst
tasking, potential Operation Thor-style targeting.
• Aggravated indicia at entry (weapon visible, balaclava, vehicle support) — upgrade to s.13 consideration;
possible armed organised group; ASU brief.
• Suspect of suspected organised crime profile — GNDOCB / DOCB interface.
• IP particularly vulnerable (elderly alone, child present, hospital-discharge case) — supervisor brief;
possible witness protection / safe-house considerations; Victims Services.

Common Pitfalls
• Letting the IP ‘clean up’ before SOCO arrives — forensic potential gone.
• Inadequate property schedule — “various jewellery” will not survive in court.
• Missing the s.12(2) variant — if the suspect entered lawfully but then committed an arrestable offence
inside, it is still burglary.
• Charging s.12 when s.13 fits — if there was a weapon at point of entry, do not under-charge.
• Failing to seize and forensic-tag the suspect’s outerwear and footwear quickly — trace evidence is lost as
the clothing is worn or laundered.

Ethics Touchpoints (2.1 Specific)
• Std 1: suspect in custody — medical, lawyer, rest, dignity in search.
• Std 2: arrest grounds documented; s.10 CJA 2006 warrant route used not s.29 OASA; force PLAN-tested.
• Std 3: IP details, photographs of home interior, property schedule — protected, shared only on need-toknow.
• Std 4: investigation depth identical regardless of IP demographic.
• Std 9: every step capable of explanation in court.

2.2 Burglary Non-Dwelling (Commercial)

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

BURGLARY NON-DWELLING: Entry of a building or part of a building that is not used as a dwelling,
as a trespasser, with intent to commit an arrestable offence (or, having so entered, committing such
offence). Contrary to s.12 CJ(T&FO)A 2001.
Commercial premises, schools, warehouses, lock-ups, GAA / sports clubhouses, places of worship, public
buildings. Lower public-policy aggravation than dwelling, but still serious. Often presents in Mountjoy district
as overnight commercial burglaries on Talbot Street, Parnell Street, North Strand. The Quilligan part-ofbuilding doctrine is alive: many “shop theft” cases are actually burglaries because the suspect entered staffonly areas.

Track A — Live
First 60 Minutes
123. Approach with awareness of possible occupation — staff still in premises, security guard, owner doing
late stock-take.
124. Cordon the building externally; ASU consideration where alarm or witness report indicates persons
inside.
125. Arrest at scene where suspect apprehended; full PROOF check.
126. Premises rep — contact owner / key-holder; they will need to attend for property check and statement.
127. Forensic considerations — commercial premises often have more entry points; identify the actual point
of entry by signs (forced lock, broken glass, ladder access, climb over).

Track B — Post-Fact
Most common pattern: business opens in the morning, finds entry overnight. Track B from the start. Owner /
manager reports. Window of incident is the close-of-business to opening time.
First Actions
128. Statement from premises rep with bracket, property schedule, points of entry as found, alarm status.
129. Walk-around photograph: all sides of premises, points of entry, internal damage and disturbance.
130. Forensic call-out: commercial burglary forensics are productive — forced entry points, drawer fronts,
till areas. Decide on SOCO.
131. CCTV: own-premises, neighbour businesses (often the most productive in commercial strips), public
realm.
132. Property: cash floats, stock, computers, branded goods. Schedule by serial / SKU where possible.
133. Linked premises / cluster: commercial burglaries cluster heavily. Search PULSE for prior offences in
area / similar MO.
COMPLIMENT POINT — LOSS PREVENTION OFFICER WHO PRESERVES SCENE
Commercial burglary scenes are often discovered by night-shift security or early-shift cleaners. The person who
recognises a scene, does not disturb it, and rings 999 with a clean account has done significant evidential work.
Action: name them in the file; if they declined to enter the premises and waited for Gardaí, that fact preserves
forensic potential. Worth a written compliment to their employer via the District Office.

Evidence Targets
• Forensic: forced entry points (frames, broken glass, lock plates), till and safe areas, computer screens,
drawer handles.
• CCTV: own + neighbour + public realm + transport.
• Alarm log: a printout / report from the alarm provider showing activation time, response, and any preactivation events (e.g. tamper alarms).
• Stock and cash: precise schedule from premises rep; till reports if relevant.
• Tools left at scene: jemmies, glass-breakers, gloves, balaclavas; bag, swag bag, discarded paperwork.

2.3 Aggravated Burglary
AGGRAVATED BURGLARY: A person who commits any burglary and at the time has with him or her
any firearm or imitation firearm, any weapon of offence, or any explosive. Contrary to s.13 CJ(T&FO)A
2001. Maximum penalty: life imprisonment.
The most serious property offence in the Act. “Weapon of offence” is widely defined (s.13(2)) — any article
made or adapted for causing injury, or intended by the person carrying it for such use. The presence of the
weapon at the time of the burglary is the aggravation — it need not be used. The mens rea is the same as for
s.12 burglary plus knowledge of the weapon at point of entry.

Track Decision
Aggravated burglary may be discovered live (suspect apprehended at scene with weapon) or post-fact (IP
describes weapon during burglary). Either way, the investigative response is at the top end — immediate
supervisor brief, possible ASU on subsequent operations, full Detective Unit engagement.

First 60 Minutes (Live)
142. Cordon and contain. ASU consideration mandatory if firearm or sustained violent indication.
143. Inhabitants: medical priority; suspect at-large information from IP.
144. Scene preservation — full forensic posture; cordon, CAP, ELOG.
145. Weapon: if recovered, exhibited under strict chain. Photograph in situ before recovery. If firearm, do
not handle without firearms-safe procedure; brief ASU or armed colleague.
146. Immediate Detective Unit / supervisor brief.
147. National crime intelligence consideration — NBCI brief for major patterns.

Evidence Targets
• Weapon — the central exhibit. Forensic submission: prints, DNA, ballistic if firearm.
• IP and witness descriptions — the weapon as seen and felt; threats made; how it was deployed.
• Forensic at scene as per dwelling burglary, intensified.
• CCTV including approach and egress with possible vehicle support.
• Phone tracing for suspect and any associates.

Suspect Strategy
Arrest under s.4 CJA 1984. May qualify for detention under s.50 CJA 2007 (organised crime) in some
circumstances — supervisor decision. Search of address under s.10 CJA 2006.

Interview Plan
Topic-disciplined; interview team typically Detective-led. The suspect’s knowledge of and intent regarding the
weapon is the unique aggravated-burglary element — distinguish carefully from possession-only or burglaryonly:

149. Did they enter the premises and was the entry permitted?
150. What did they carry on the entry?

Aggravated burglary calls are among the most psychologically demanding for the responding Garda. A colleague
