Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 3 — Robbery
Robbery is theft plus force or threat of force. The distinction between robbery and theft is the central operational
issue — get it wrong and you under-charge a maximum-life offence, or over-charge a maximum-ten-year
offence. Robbery is one of the most psychologically damaging offences for the IP after dwelling burglary —
the victim is confronted directly. Investigative response must reflect that.

3.0 Robbery — Foundations
ROBBERY: A person is guilty of robbery if he or she steals, and immediately before or at the time of
doing so, and in order to do so, uses force on any person or puts or seeks to put any person in fear of
being then and there subjected to force. Contrary to s.14 Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences)
Act 2001. Maximum penalty: life imprisonment.

The Three Elements
160. Theft — all five theft elements (s.4) must be present. No theft, no robbery.
161. Force, or threat of force — used on any person (not necessarily the property owner; can be a bystander)
immediately before or at the time of the theft.
162. In order to steal — the force must be in order to commit, or facilitate the commission of, the theft.
Force used after the theft is complete (e.g. to escape) is not robbery, though may be assault.

Key Authority
DPP v Hughes [2012] IECCA 69 — Force Element
Holding: Force must be more than minimal contact incidental to a snatch. The grade of force required is a
matter of fact for the jury; pushing, jerking, striking, threatening with a weapon all qualify.
Why It Matters: Snatch theft is a frequent classification problem. If contact is purely incidental (bag-snap that
catches the IP’s arm), it may be theft from person. If the suspect pulls the IP forward, knocks them off-balance,
or pushes them, it is robbery.
R v Dawson [1976] (English authority — persuasive)
Holding: Even minor force used to overcome resistance is sufficient. The use of force need not cause injury.
Why It Matters: Cited approvingly in Irish courts. The jostling or shouldering of an IP to facilitate a pocketdip can suffice.
DPP v Coffey [2008] IECCA — Threat of Force
Holding: Implied threat — producing a weapon, threatening language, hostile posture — suffices. The IP’s
subjective fear must be reasonable in the circumstances.
Why It Matters: IP statement must capture the threat as perceived: what was said, what was shown, what they
feared, why their fear was reasonable.
Salduz / Gormley line
As with all serious offences, lawyer access at first interrogation is constitutionally and Convention-required.
See Case Law Appendix.

Ethics Touchpoints (Foundations)
• Std 4: IP trauma is significant; victim engagement (Garda Victim Liaison Office) must be offered, not
assumed.
• Std 1: suspects in custody for robbery often present with complex needs (addiction, mental health, possible
juvenile status). Full custody safeguards.
• Std 7: serious-offence investigation must withstand scrutiny end-to-end. Speak up if you spot a corner being
cut.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3.1 Robbery (Simple)
ROBBERY: Theft accompanied by force, or threat of force, used immediately before or at the time of
the theft, in order to steal. Contrary to s.14 CJ(T&FO)A 2001.
Inner-city Dublin pattern: street robbery (mugging) of pedestrians, transport-hub robberies, taxi-driver
robberies, doorstep robberies of vulnerable persons, mobile-phone snatches with violence. High Track A
potential because IP usually reports immediately.

Track A — Live Incident
First 60 Minutes
163. Treat IP — medical assessment, however minor injury. The contact may be the central evidence point.
Photograph injuries at scene if IP consents.
164. Suspect description circulated within 5 minutes. Include direction of flight, vehicle if any. Public-order
risk if multiple suspects — brief incoming units.
165. Cordon at the scene to preserve any discarded items (weapons, IP property, suspect clothing).
166. Witness identification at scene — anyone who saw the encounter.
167. CCTV — wide. The robbery itself, the approach, the flight, possible vehicle. Multi-source preservation
request within shift.
168. Detective Unit brief — every robbery warrants Detective brief at minimum; serious cases warrant
Detective-led.
169. Forensic consideration — if force involved physical contact (grappling, weapon contact), Garda
preservation of clothing for trace evidence on both IP and any apprehended suspect.
Scene Management
The scene is the encounter location. Cordon if any forensic potential exists — blood, weapon, discarded
property. Photograph in situ. Common Approach Path if cordon required. IP’s clothing may be evidential (DNA
transfer from suspect grappling) — consider preserving.
Evidence Targets
• IP statement — full sensory account: what they saw, heard, felt, said. Verbatim of suspect’s words where
possible. Property taken (schedule with values, serials, photos of similar).
• IP injuries — medical statement, GP / hospital records, photographs.
• Witness statements — anyone present, ADVOKATE-assessed.
• CCTV — broad bracket; multi-camera reconstruction of approach, encounter, flight.
• Forensic — weapon, discarded clothing, trace on IP / suspect.
• Phone tracing — if IP’s phone stolen, IMEI blacklist + tracking attempt; cell-site for suspect if known.
• Suspect property — full search; cash, phones, IP’s described items, weapon, suspect clothing matching
CCTV.
Witness Strategy
• IP: primary. Allow for shock; supplementary statement at 24–48 hours. Treat with dignity — they have
just been assaulted and robbed.
• Direct witnesses: ADVOKATE the ID account; statement of what they saw, heard, felt of the threat.
• Medical staff: GP, A&E doctor — statement re injury, mechanism described.
• Recovery witnesses: if property recovered through pawnshop, second-hand sale, third party — chain
witnesses for each transaction.
COMPLIMENT POINT — IP CARE AT SCENE
A Garda who takes time to calm and reassure a traumatised robbery IP at the scene, who briefs them on next
steps, and who hands off cleanly to Victim Liaison has done foundational work — both for the case (a calm IP
gives a better statement) and for the institutional relationship with victims.
Action: name them in the file. Strong-IP-engagement contributions sometimes warrant a Commissioner’s

Less common in robbery than in other property offences — IPs usually report fast. But it occurs: tourist returns

174. Forensic — reduced potential, not zero. IP clothing may still hold trace; recovered property may still

175. PULSE — detailed; flag for Detective brief.

Defence Will Attack
Typical Attack Vectors
182. Force element — “My client did not touch the IP; this is theft from person at most.” The classic robberyvs-theft challenge.
183. In order to steal — “My client argued with the IP; theft was opportunistic and incidental.”
184. Identification — IP under stress; described someone in dark clothing; CCTV image inconclusive.
185. IP reliability — intoxication, prior knowledge of suspect, possible motive to lie (e.g. drug debt).
186. Joint enterprise — multiple suspects, defence carves up roles to fit each into lesser offence.

Appellate Authority Where Defence Won
• DPP v Hughes [2012] IECCA 69 — force element scrutinised; minimal-contact convictions vulnerable.
• DPP v O’Reilly [2009] IECCA 18 — sole CCTV ID with poor image quality insufficient.
• DPP v Maguire [2008] IECCA — absent formal ID procedure, dock ID very weak.
• Salduz v Turkey [GC] 2008 — admissions inadmissible if lawyer access denied.
• DPP v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17 — admissions excluded where pre-consultation interview.
How to Bullet-Proof
• Force: IP statement positively articulates the contact / threat — not assumed. Medical evidence of injury
where present. Witness corroboration.
• Connection between force and theft: IP statement on the sequence (“he pushed me to take the bag”, not “he
pushed me then took the bag”).
• ID: formal procedure (parade / VIPER) wherever possible; ADVOKATE statement; corroboration by
CCTV / forensic / property recovery.
• Custody: lawyer access strict; cautions clear; inference warnings statutorily precise.
• Mens rea: probe claim of right early in interview; document the suspect’s account of ownership.

• Weapon used or implied — immediate supervisor brief; consider firearms charges; ASU brief for any

• Series of robberies (MO match) — District tasking, Detective-led, possible cross-divisional analyst.
• Vulnerable IP (elderly, disability, child) — senior supervisor brief; Victim Liaison engagement; possible
Operation Aimsir-style targeted protection.
• Foreign or tourist IP — District Office for embassy liaison; statement and departure-window planning.

• Taxi driver / delivery driver targeted — association liaison; brief professional body if pattern.

Common Pitfalls
• Charging theft when force was used — under-charge that the DPP may direct upgrade on, wasting time.
• Charging robbery when no force was used — over-charge that the trial judge will direct out, embarrassing
the file.
• Failing to seize the IP’s clothing where physical contact occurred — forensic opportunity lost.
• Omitting the medical statement — injury is evidence of force; missing it is a gap.
• Doorstep questioning before caution — incriminating answers vulnerable.

• Std 1 — custody safeguards intensified given gravity.

3.2 Robbery Aggravated by Weapon Use
ROBBERY WITH WEAPON: Robbery (s.14) where a firearm, imitation firearm, or weapon of offence
is produced or used in the commission. Charged as s.14 robbery with separate counts under the Firearms
Acts and / or s.11 Firearms and Offensive Weapons Act 1990; the weapon element aggravates sentence.
Operationally, the highest-end property crime alongside aggravated burglary. Bank, betting shop, post office,
jewellery shop, pharmacy targets in inner-city Dublin. Tiger robberies (kidnap-coerced theft) belong here also.
Always Detective-led, often GNDOCB / Special Detective Unit / Armed Support Unit involvement.

188. Cordon outer perimeter — broad. ASU activation if firearm involvement reported.

Appellate Authority Where Defence Won

How to Bullet-Proof
• Joint enterprise: build the role-by-role evidence (communications, position at scene, post-incident conduct).
• ID: every available corroborative evidence stream (voice, gait, possessions, vehicle, phones).
• Forensic: rigorous chain; SOCO statements; integrity of transfer; controlled samples.
• Custody: every action documented and time-stamped.

Aggravated robbery is itself a red flag. Every such case operates from the top: Superintendent brief, Detective

• Charging robbery alone where firearm charges should run in parallel — the firearm count carries discrete

• Failing to brief National Firearms Unit on ballistics.
• Inadequate joint-enterprise evidence build — wider net cases collapse on this.
• Disclosure failures — catastrophic; case-ending.

3.3 Attempted Robbery
ATTEMPTED ROBBERY: Attempt to commit robbery — act more than merely preparatory, with the
mens rea of robbery, but the offence not completed. S.14 CJ(T&FO)A 2001 plus common-law attempt;
same penalty as the substantive offence.
Frequent operational scenario: suspect approaches IP with intent and demand, IP resists or escapes, no property

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

taken. Force may or may not have been used. Investigative posture is identical to completed robbery — do not
down-tool because the offence “did not succeed.” The IP’s trauma is the same; the offender’s dangerousness is
the same.

Element Analysis
Attempt requires (a) the mens rea for robbery and (b) an act more than merely preparatory — i.e. the suspect
has crossed the threshold from planning into execution. The classic Irish authority is The People (AG) v
Thornton [1952] IR 91 (and the more recent application in DPP v Bell [2015] IECA 240).

Investigation
As robbery completed — IP statement, scene, witnesses, CCTV, forensic where contact occurred, suspect
strategy, interview plan. The interview must additionally address mens rea (what they intended) and act-notcompleted (why they stopped — resisted, interrupted, voluntarily desisted — each has different consequence).

Chapter 4 — Criminal Damage
Criminal damage covers the breaking, defacing, vandalising, burning, and otherwise impairing of property. It
ranges from a graffiti tag on a wall to arson of an inhabited dwelling. The Criminal Damage Act 1991 (CDA)
governs. Volume crime in inner-city Dublin; serious cases (arson, damage with intent to endanger life) demand
full Detective posture.

4.0 Criminal Damage — Foundations
CRIMINAL DAMAGE: A person who without lawful excuse damages any property belonging to another
intending to damage any such property or being reckless as to whether such property would be damaged
is guilty of an offence. Contrary to s.2(1) Criminal Damage Act 1991.

The Elements
211. Damages — includes any act causing impairment of value or utility. The damage need not be
permanent.
212. Property — includes land, buildings, vehicles, animals (tamed or kept), and intangible property in
certain circumstances.
213. Belonging to another — the property must belong to a person other than the accused (or to the accused
with others).
214. Without lawful excuse — s.6 CDA 1991 sets out specific defences (consent, belief in consent,
protection of property).
215. Mens rea — intention to damage, or recklessness as to whether damage would result.

Key Authority
DPP v Murray [1977] IR 360 — Recklessness
Holding: Recklessness involves conscious disregard of an obvious and serious risk. The accused must have
actually adverted to the risk; objective recklessness alone is insufficient.
Why It Matters: Interview must probe what the accused was aware of — not what a reasonable person should
have known. “Did you realise there was a risk that…?”
DPP v Cagney and McGrath [2008] 2 IR 111 — Recklessness in Practice
Holding: Reaffirmed Murray standard. Subjective awareness of the risk required.
Why It Matters: Particularly important for arson cases where the issue is whether the accused appreciated the
risk to inhabitants.
R v Hancock and Shankland [1986] AC 455 (HL — persuasive)
Holding: Foresight of a virtually certain consequence amounts to intention. The greater the probability the
accused foresaw, the stronger the inference of intent.
Why It Matters: Useful in serious cases (arson with intent) where intent must be proved circumstantially.

Ethics Touchpoints (Foundations)
• Std 3 — victim impact often understated for criminal damage; treat the IP as a victim of significant
intrusion.
• Std 4 — graffiti, low-value damage, repeat offenders — same investigative threshold as higher-profile
cases.

4.1 Simple Criminal Damage (s.2(1) CDA 1991)
SIMPLE CRIMINAL DAMAGE: Damaging property of another without lawful excuse, intentionally or
recklessly. Contrary to s.2(1) Criminal Damage Act 1991. Maximum: 10 years on indictment.

4.2 Arson
ARSON: Criminal damage by fire — destroying or damaging property of another by fire, intentionally
or recklessly. Contrary to s.2(3) CDA 1991 (simple arson — max 10 yrs) or s.2(2) (arson with intent to
endanger life or being reckless as to whether life is endangered — max life).
Critical operational distinction: simple arson (s.2(3), max 10 yrs) vs. arson endangering life (s.2(2), max life).
The s.2(2) variant requires the additional mens rea — intention or recklessness as to endangerment of life.
Inhabited dwelling fires almost always engage s.2(2); commercial premises out-of-hours typically s.2(3).

First 60 Minutes (Live)
238. Immediate priority: life. Evacuation, occupants, neighbours. Coordinate with Dublin Fire Brigade.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

239. Cordon outer perimeter for both safety and forensic preservation.
240. Suspect — if on scene or fleeing, arrest under s.4 CJA 1984. Forensic preservation of suspect clothing

242. Scenes of Crime call-out — fire scene investigation; petrol / accelerant samples; ignition device
recovery.
243. CCTV — broad; approach, flight, possible vehicle, possible accelerant carrying.
244. Detective Unit brief immediately.

Track B — Post-Fact (Fire Already Extinguished When Garda Notified)
Some arson is reported only after the fire is out. The scene is heavily disrupted by suppression. Forensic
preservation must still be attempted, working closely with Fire Investigation.
First Actions
245. Liaise with Fire Brigade Officer — cause-and-origin findings.
246. Scene preservation — establish cordon even after suppression; identify seat of fire; protect.
247. Witnesses — anyone who saw / heard prior to fire, anyone who saw the fire start, anyone who saw
persons leaving.
248. Accelerant testing — SOCO samples; submission to Forensic Science Ireland.

• Fire scene — seat of fire, accelerant traces, ignition device, multiple seats (indicator of deliberate setting).
• Fire Brigade Officer’s report — expert evidence of cause and origin.
• CCTV — approach with carrier (jerry can, bottle), the act, the flight.
• Phone / digital — communications, mapping, purchases (accelerant from a petrol station).
• Suspect clothing — trace evidence of accelerant, fire debris.
• Recovered accelerant container — prints, DNA, source-tracing.
• CCTV from petrol stations — accelerant purchase pre-fire.

Detective-led. Custody under s.4 (or s.50 CJA 2007 if organised feature). Address search under s.10 CJA 2006.
Forensic preservation of suspect from arrest — clothing, hands, swabs.

4.3 Threats to Damage (s.3 CDA 1991)
THREATS TO DAMAGE: A person who threatens, with intention that the threat shall be carried out,
to damage property belonging to another, or to damage his or her own property in a way which is likely
to endanger the life of another, is guilty of an offence. Contrary to s.3 CDA 1991. Maximum: 10 years.
Often appears as an add-on to other offences (DV intimidation, public order incidents, gang feuds) but stands
alone where the threat is the central wrong. Important: the threat must be intended to be taken seriously — a
rhetorical “I’ll burn this place down” in anger may not satisfy.

Investigation
260. IP statement — verbatim of the threat, context, IP’s perception of seriousness, prior history with
suspect.
261. Witnesses to the threat.
262. Means evidence — does the suspect possess the means to carry out the threat (accelerant found, history
of damage).
263. Communications evidence — threats often in text, WhatsApp, social media; preserve and screenshot
with metadata.
264. Suspect interview — admission, denial, claimed jest or hyperbole; intent probed.

Charge Selection
Charge under s.3 CDA 1991. Consider parallel charges: harassment (s.10 NFOAP 1997), coercion (s.9 NFOAP
1997), domestic violence offences.

Defence Will Attack
• Intent — “My client was venting, no real intent.”
• Seriousness — IP overreaction, not a credible threat.
• Communication integrity — contested authorship of texts / posts.
How to Bullet-Proof
• IP statement on seriousness factors — history of damage by suspect, context, capability of suspect.
• Communications: provenance documented; account verification; screenshots time-stamped with platform
metadata where possible.
• Witness corroboration of context.

Cross-References
See: Harassment (s.10 NFOAP 1997 — Vol 2); Coercion (s.9 NFOAP 1997); DV offences (Vol 4); Criminal
Damage (4.1).

Chapter 5 — Handling and Possession of Stolen Property
Handling and possession of stolen property are the downstream offences of theft and burglary. They catch the
receivers, the resellers, and the persons found with stolen items. They are also operational fallback charges
where the upstream theft is unproveable but possession is clear.

5.0 Foundations
HANDLING: Receiving, retaining, removing, disposing of, or assisting in the disposal of, stolen property
knowing or believing it to be stolen. Contrary to s.17 CJ(T&FO)A 2001 — max 10 yrs indict.
POSSESSION OF STOLEN PROPERTY: Possessing stolen property knowing or believing it to be stolen
— the more limited offence. Contrary to s.18 CJ(T&FO)A 2001 — max 5 yrs indict.

The Key Element — Knowledge or Belief
The mens rea is knowing or believing. “Believing” is wider than knowing — it captures wilful blindness and
the “must have known” situations. Suspicion alone (“I thought it might be stolen”) is not enough.

Key Authority
DPP v Brennan [1998] 4 IR 67 — Recent Possession Inference
Already cited in Chapters 1 and 2 and in the Case Law Appendix. Central to handling: unexplained recent
possession of stolen property founds an inference of either theft or handling. Strong tool for the prosecution.
R v Forsyth [1997] 2 Cr App R 299 (UK — persuasive)
Holding: Belief that goods were stolen can be inferred from the price paid (suspiciously low), the circumstances
of acquisition (pub car park rather than retail), and the absence of paperwork.
Why It Matters: Interview must probe the acquisition circumstances in detail — these are the building blocks
of belief.
Atwal v Massey [1971] 3 All ER 881 (UK — persuasive)
Holding: “Belief” means more than suspicion; it is acceptance that the goods are stolen even without certain
knowledge.
Why It Matters: Belief is built circumstantially; interview must accumulate the indicators.

Ethics Touchpoints (Foundations)
• Std 3 — second-hand-trade investigation involves sensitive customer / business data; handle with care.
• Std 4 — the pawnshop operator co-operating with Gardaí is a partner, not a suspect; treat as such until
evidence otherwise.

5.1 Handling (s.17 CJ(T&FO)A 2001)
HANDLING: Otherwise than in the course of the stealing, dishonestly receives, undertakes the retention,
removal, disposal, or realisation by or for the benefit of another, or arranges to do so, of stolen property,
knowing or believing it to be stolen. Contrary to s.17 CJ(T&FO)A 2001.
Captures the full chain of disposal after a theft. Inner-city Dublin patterns: pawnshops receiving stolen jewellery
/ watches / electronics; pubs and house-parties as exchange points; online resale (Adverts.ie, Facebook
Marketplace, DoneDeal); cross-border resale; cigarette and alcohol distribution networks.

Track — Mostly Track B
Handling is rarely caught live. Usually the investigation begins with an item recovered, traced back to a theft,
and the question becomes who handled it between thief and recovery point.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Investigation Strategy
265. Start at recovery point — statement of the recovery witness (pawnshop operator, buyer, finder).
266. Work backwards — the handler is the person between the thief and this point. Interview the recovery
witness on who they got it from.
267. Documentary trail — pawnshop ticket (Pawnbrokers Act 1964 retention requirements), online listing
screenshots, message threads.
268. Forensic on the item — fingerprints of intermediate handlers.
269. Phone / digital — communications between thief and handler; payment records.
270. Cash trail — deposits, withdrawals.

Evidence Targets
• The stolen item — traced from original IP through every intermediate handler to recovery.
• Pawnshop / second-hand records — mandatory retention under Pawnbrokers Act 1964 and Second-Hand
Goods regulations.
• Online listings — archived; sellercheckers; platform legal-process material.
• Communications — texts, social-media messages, voice messages.
• Payments — bank records, cash.
• Witnesses to the transaction — anyone present, anyone subsequently aware.

Suspect Strategy
Handling is arrestable. S.4 CJA 1984 detention. Search of premises under s.10 CJA 2006 — often productive
(handlers tend to accumulate inventory).

Chapter 6 — Unauthorised Taking of Motor Propelled
Vehicle
UTMV (sometimes referred to operationally as TADA — “taking and driving away”) covers the taking of
motor vehicles without owner consent for a temporary purpose. Distinct from theft of a vehicle (where the mens
rea includes permanent deprivation). High-volume in inner-city Dublin; often committed by juveniles, often
paired with criminal damage, dangerous driving, and pursuit incidents.

6.0 Foundations
UTMV: A person who takes a mechanically propelled vehicle without the consent of the owner or other
lawful authority, or who knowing that a vehicle has been so taken, drives or is carried in it without such
consent or authority, is guilty of an offence. Contrary to s.112(1) Road Traffic Act 1961 (as amended).
Maximum penalty: 12 months / Class A fine on summary; broader for aggravated forms.

Two Statutory Routes
UTMV may also be charged as theft under s.4 CJ(T&FO)A 2001 where the evidence supports permanentdeprivation intent. The Cleary doctrine (extended permanent deprivation — see Ch.1 and Case Law Appendix)
is relevant: pawning a vehicle, abandoning in another county, fitting false plates, etc., may all evidence
permanent-deprivation intent.

The Elements (s.112 RTA 1961)
288. Mechanically propelled vehicle — broadly defined; includes cars, vans, motorcycles, mopeds.
289. Takes — actual taking; mere unauthorised use (e.g. of an employer’s vehicle within continuing
permission) may not be a taking.
290. Without consent of owner or lawful authority — consent issue often arises in family / partner contexts.
291. Knowing-and-carried variant — the passenger who knew the vehicle was unlawfully taken is also
caught.

Key Authority
DPP v Cleary [2007] IECCA — Permanent Deprivation
Already cited in Case Law Appendix. Critical for the UTMV-to-theft upgrade. If the suspect’s dealings with
the vehicle effectively eliminate the owner’s use or value, theft under s.4 may be the right charge.
DPP v O’Loughlin [1979] IR 85 — “Taking”
Holding: Taking requires asportation — actual movement of the vehicle. Simply sitting in it is not enough; the
vehicle must be moved.
Why It Matters: Where suspect found inside vehicle but engine off, key not turned, no movement — charge
fits more naturally as attempt or interference, not the substantive UTMV.

Ethics Touchpoints
• Std 1 — UTMV suspects often juveniles; full juvenile safeguards (responsible adult, JLO, Diversion
Programme assessment).
• Std 4 — same investigative depth regardless of suspect age / background.
• Std 2 — vehicle pursuit decisions: Pursuit Policy must be followed; PLAN test on use of force.

6.1 UTMV — Operational Investigation
UTMV: Taking a mechanically propelled vehicle without the consent of the owner. Contrary to s.112(1)
Road Traffic Act 1961 (as amended).

First 60 Minutes

293. Suspect arrest — PROOF check; s.4 CJA 1984 if theft / aggravated form; otherwise summary arrest
under s.49 (drink-driving) or other applicable powers.
294. Vehicle preservation — in-place photography; recovery to PEMS-secured location.
295. Owner contact and identification of vehicle as theirs; statement of consent / non-consent.
296. Forensic potential — ignition area (hot-wire damage), prints on steering wheel / gear lever / handbrake.
297. Damage assessment — separate criminal damage charges where applicable.
Live Recovery Scene
If the vehicle is found abandoned with engine running / recently used, treat as a live recovery: photograph in
situ, dust for prints, recover discarded items in the vehicle.

Track B — Post-Fact (Vehicle Reported Missing, Recovered Later)
Owner returns to where vehicle was parked, finds it gone. Reports as theft / unauthorised taking. Vehicle may
turn up days or weeks later, often damaged or burnt out.

305. Owner attendance — statement on damage and missing items; restoration of vehicle.
306. ANPR analysis — movements during missing period.
307. Pattern checks — UTMVs in district / division for cluster matching.

Evidence Targets
• The vehicle itself — forensic, damage assessment, ANPR trail.
• Owner statement on non-consent and ownership.
• CCTV — taking, route, abandonment.
• ANPR — missing-period reconstruction.
• Suspect evidence — vehicle keys / tools (programming devices, relay attack equipment), clothing matching
CCTV.
• Witness evidence — anyone who saw the taking or subsequent use.

Suspect Strategy
Arrestable under s.49(8) Road Traffic Act 1961 (s.107 RTA in operation), or under s.4 CJA 1984 if theft charge
in contemplation. Where suspect is a minor, JLO immediate; Diversion Programme assessment.

308. Possession and use of the vehicle — admit / deny.
309. Authority — claimed permission from owner; any prior permission history.

COMPLIMENT POINT — PURSUIT DISCIPLINE
A pursuit-trained Garda who disengages a pursuit when policy requires (public risk too high) demonstrates

Action: pursuit decisions should be documented in the pursuit log and the Garda’s decision rationale recorded.
Where the disengagement prevented a foreseeable harm, the Garda’s judgement merits explicit recognition in the
post-incident review.

• Std 1 — juvenile suspect safeguards (responsible adult, JLO, Diversion).
• Std 2 — pursuit policy adherence; PLAN test on any use of force; TPAC tactics within authorisation.
• Std 8 — pursuit decision documented contemporaneously.

• Charging UTMV when theft fits (permanent deprivation evident in conduct).
• Failing to forensic the driver-position controls before owner repossesses the vehicle.
• Omitting the owner’s positive statement of non-consent and authorised-driver list.
• Not running passenger / knowing-and-carried charges separately.

Chapter 7 — Deception and Fraud
Deception offences are the white-collar / financial end of property crime. Operationally for a Mountjoy Garda,
the common variants are: making gain or causing loss by deception (s.6), false accounting (s.10), and the use
of false instruments. Larger or more complex frauds are referred to the Garda National Economic Crime Bureau
(GNECB) — but the first responder is often a uniformed Garda taking the initial report at a station counter.
This chapter focuses on the operational level: identifying what offence is engaged, preserving evidence early,
and producing a file that GNECB can pick up cleanly.

7.0 Foundations
The CJ(T&FO)A 2001 codified the deception offences and replaced the older Larceny Acts framework. The
Act provides a graduated suite of offences from making-gain-by-deception (s.6) through false-accounting
(s.10), unlawful use of computer (s.9), and beyond.

Common Deception Patterns Encountered
• Cheque fraud — dishonoured cheques, forged signatures, stolen chequebooks.
• Card fraud — stolen card use, online card-not-present fraud, ATM skimming.
• Online fraud — invoice redirection, romance fraud, fake-investment fraud, business email compromise.
• Identity fraud — impersonation to open accounts, take out loans, access services.
• Distraction / doorstep fraud — targeting elderly, often paired with theft from dwelling.
• Employee fraud / embezzlement — see Ch.1.4.

Key Authority
DPP v Murray and Murray [1994] 1 IR 78 — Deception
Holding: Deception requires a misrepresentation — by words or conduct — made with intent to deceive, where
the misrepresentation was operative in causing the gain or loss.
Why It Matters: Build the file in three pieces: (a) the representation made by the suspect, (b) its falsity, (c) the
IP’s reliance on it.
DPP v Ennis [2002] IECCA — Causation
Holding: The deception must be a substantial cause of the transfer of property or gain. The IP must have acted
(at least in part) because of the deception.
Why It Matters: IP statement must articulate the role of the deception in their decision-making.

Ethics Touchpoints
• Std 3 — victims often distressed and embarrassed (especially in romance / investment fraud);
confidentiality and dignity essential.
• Std 4 — elderly fraud victims sometimes blamed for naivety; reject this; same investigative depth as any
other crime.
• Std 9 — financial trails are technical; document the investigative method carefully for court.

7.1 Making Gain or Causing Loss by Deception (s.6 CJ(T&FO)A 2001)
MAKING GAIN OR CAUSING LOSS BY DECEPTION: A person who dishonestly, with the intention
of making a gain for himself or another, or of causing loss to another, by any deception induces another
to do or refrain from doing an act, is guilty of an offence. Contrary to s.6 CJ(T&FO)A 2001 — max 5 yrs
indict.

Elements
323. Dishonestly — same as s.2(1) definition.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

324. With intention of making gain / causing loss — gain or loss in money or other property.
325. By any deception — statement or conduct, including by omission where a duty to disclose exists.
326. Induces — the IP must have been induced to act / refrain from acting.

Track — Almost Always Track B
Fraud is reported after the fact — the IP discovers the loss / has been deceived / receives the dishonoured cheque
/ spots the unauthorised transactions on a statement. Cold investigation discipline applies throughout.

First Actions on Report
327. Sit the IP down. Get a calm, detailed account.
328. Establish: the misrepresentation, when it was made, how, by whom, in what channel.
329. Preserve communications immediately — emails, texts, messages, screenshots with platform / sender
metadata, voicemails.
330. Identify the loss — amount transferred, by what means (cheque, transfer, card, cash, cryptocurrency).
331. Financial preservation — IP’s bank may freeze the receiving account if alerted quickly; this is the most
time-critical step. “Fraud bureau” hotlines exist at all major Irish banks; IP’s action, your prompt.
332. PULSE incident — Deception (with specific sub-category if available).
333. Decide referral threshold — below threshold (typically simple, sub-€10k, single-victim): investigate
locally; above threshold or complex (multi-jurisdictional, large sums, organised): refer GNECB.

Evidence Targets
• Communications — every message, email, social-media exchange between IP and suspect. Provenance,
metadata, account details.
• Banking records — IP’s account showing the transfer; receiving account details; subsequent flow if
traceable.
• Identification of the suspect — may be only a digital identity (account number, email, phone, social profile);
requires production order to platforms.
• IP statement — detailed; deception narrative; reliance; decision-making explained.
• Documentary fraud — dishonoured cheques, forged signatures, false invoices, false documents.

Suspect Strategy
Arrestable. S.4 CJA 1984 detention available. Suspect identification is usually the central challenge in
deception cases. Search of address under s.10 CJA 2006 — often productive for devices, documents, bank
records.

Interview Plan
334. Identification — do they admit being the person who made the communications.
335. Representation — the words / conduct put to them; admit or deny making the statement; if admit, was
it true.
336. Intent — did they intend to deceive.
337. Gain / loss — what did they do with the funds, who else benefited.
338. Pattern — if a series, careful linking questioning.
339. Inference questions on unaccounted-for items / funds.

PULSE / PEMS / File Index
PULSE category: Deception / Fraud (specific sub-category).
Key file additions: Banking print-outs as exhibits with statement of bank official; communications product
with continuity statement; suspect digital-identity material.

• Causation: IP statement explicit on the role of the representation in the decision.
• Continuity: digital evidence chain documented — collection, transfer, storage, integrity (hash values where

Red Flag Escalation
• Sums above operational threshold — refer GNECB.

• Multiple victims, organised pattern — GNECB.
• Cross-border — GNECB; mutual legal assistance routes.
• Cryptocurrency feature — GNECB Cyber Crime Bureau.
• Victim is vulnerable (elderly, disability) — sustained engagement; specialised support.
COMPLIMENT POINT — EARLY BANK ALERT
The Garda who, on first hearing the IP’s account at the counter, immediately prompts the IP to call their bank
fraud line is doing the single most important act in fraud recovery. The first hour is the only window during
which funds can usually be recalled. Many recoveries trace back to this prompt.
Action: note the prompt in the cover report. It is a small thing that may have saved the IP six-figure sums.

• Std 3 — victim embarrassment in romance / investment fraud is often acute; sensitive handling.
• Std 4 — reject victim-blaming attitudes (“how did they fall for this?”); the offence is the suspect’s, not the
IP’s.
• Std 8 — financial / digital investigations are technical; ask for help where needed, do not bluff.

Common Pitfalls
• Missing the early bank alert window.
• Allowing the IP to delete communications or close accounts before evidence is captured.
• Charging deception when forgery / false instrument / identity theft is the better fit.
• Failing to brief GNECB where threshold is crossed.
• Treating digital evidence informally — screenshots without metadata, no continuity statement.

7.2 False Accounting (s.10 CJ(T&FO)A 2001)
FALSE ACCOUNTING: Dishonest falsification, destruction, defacing, concealing, or producing of any
account or any record required for an accounting purpose with intent to gain, cause loss, or to procure
a person to do or refrain from acting. Contrary to s.10 CJ(T&FO)A 2001 — max 5 yrs indict.

Where It Bites
Employee theft cases (Ch.1.4) frequently engage s.10 in parallel — the employee falsifies till receipts, expenses,
stock records, or accounting entries to conceal the theft. Independent contractor fraud, bookkeeping fraud, and
corporate financial-statement fraud may also engage.

Investigation
345. Document trail — the records as they exist (original) and as they should exist (control records, system
logs, audit trail).
346. Statement of the system / records custodian — how records are kept, who has access, integrity controls.
347. Forensic accounting analysis — GNECB referral if scale / complexity warrants.
348. Suspect interview — acknowledgment of the records, awareness of falsification.

Appendix — Case Law Resource
This appendix collects the leading authorities cited across the volume in one place, with concise vLex-style
summaries (Facts / Holding / Significance / Why It Matters in Your Investigation). Each case is also referenced
inline within the relevant incident chapter; this appendix is the single point of look-up.
Sources: Irish reports (Supreme Court, Court of Appeal, former Court of Criminal Appeal, High Court),
European Court of Human Rights (Strasbourg), and Court of Justice of the European Union (Luxembourg).
vLex Ireland (vlex.com) is a reliable database for full judgments — the citations below are sufficient to locate
each case there or on courts.ie / hudoc.echr.coe.int.
Verification reminder: case law is alive. Before relying on any authority in court, check it has not been
distinguished, doubted, or overruled. The headnote summaries here are operational guidance, not legal advice.

Part A — Irish Authority
Arrest, Search, and Seizure
DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31 — The Exclusionary Rule (Modern Test)
Facts: Search warrant obtained under s.29 of the Offences Against the State Act 1939 (later struck down in
Damache, below). JC sought exclusion of evidence on the basis the warrant was invalid.
Holding: The Supreme Court (5–2 majority) reformulated the exclusionary rule. Evidence obtained in breach
of constitutional rights is no longer automatically excluded. The trial judge must consider whether the breach
was (a) inadvertent, (b) deliberate and conscious. Inadvertent breach: discretionary exclusion. Deliberate and
conscious: presumptive exclusion. The prosecution bears the onus of justifying admission.
Significance: Replaced the strict Kenny / Healy rule. The investigative member’s state of mind at the time of
the breach is now central.
Why It Matters: Document your reasoning at the time of every warrant application, every search, every
seizure. If a later defect is discovered, you want your notebook to evidence good faith — not afterthought
rationalisation.
DPP v Damache [2012] IESC 11 — Invalidity of s.29 OASA Warrants
Facts: Search warrant under s.29 OASA 1939 issued by a Superintendent who was part of the investigation.
Constitutional challenge.
Holding: The Supreme Court held that a warrant issued by a person who is part of the investigating team
violates the independent and impartial issuing-authority requirement implicit in Article 40.5 of the Constitution.
S.29 was struck down.
Significance: Warrants must be issued by an authority independent of the investigation — typically a District
Judge or, for limited s.10 CJA 2006 applications, a Peace Commissioner.
Why It Matters: Always check the warrant route. For property crime, the workhorse warrant is s.10 CJA 2006
(search of premises in connection with an arrestable offence) on application to a District Judge. Do not be
tempted by shortcuts.
DPP v Cunningham [2012] IECCA 64 — Application of the Exclusionary Rule
Facts: Search uncovered evidence; defence challenged on warrant defect.
Holding: Pre-JC, applied the strict exclusionary rule. Post-JC, would now be analysed under the
inadvertent/deliberate test.
Why It Matters: Comparator case for understanding the shift in the law. Still cited for the proposition that the
State carries the onus once a breach is established.
DPP v Madden [1977] IR 336 — Voluntariness of Admissions
Facts: Admissions made by suspect during detention; oppression alleged.
Holding: Admissions are admissible only if made voluntarily. The trial judge must conduct a voir dire if
voluntariness is challenged. Oppression, inducement, or threats vitiate voluntariness.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Why It Matters: Custody record completeness, rest periods, refreshments, medical attention — all evidenced
on the record because each is a building block of voluntariness.
DPP v Walsh [1980] IR 294 — Reasonable Grounds for Arrest
Facts: Arrest challenged for lack of objective grounds.
Holding: The arresting member must have an honest suspicion based on objective grounds, contemporaneously
formed and articulable. Subjective belief is insufficient if it cannot be objectively defended.
Why It Matters: Your notebook entry of the moment of arrest must record the grounds. “I had reasonable
suspicion” is not enough — say why.

Identification
DPP v O’Reilly [2009] IECCA 18 — CCTV Identification
Facts: Conviction relied substantially on CCTV image of poor quality with no live identification.
Holding: Conviction quashed. Where CCTV is the sole or primary source of identification and image quality
is poor, conviction is unsafe without corroboration.
Why It Matters: Improve CCTV evidence at source where possible — better cameras, multi-angle. Where
image quality is fixed, build corroboration (clothing recovered, IMEI matching, suspect admissions on
incidental facts).
DPP v Maguire [2008] IECCA — Dock Identification
Facts: Dock identification of accused by IP without prior formal ID procedure.
Holding: Dock identification is the weakest form of ID evidence. Where a formal ID procedure (parade or
VIPER) could have been conducted and was not, dock ID should be approached with extreme caution.
Why It Matters: Always offer a formal ID procedure where the witness has not previously identified the
suspect. Document the offer and any refusal.
DPP v Boyce [2008] IESC 62 — Turnbull Warning
Facts: Disputed visual identification by a single witness.
Holding: Adopted the Turnbull caution: trial judge must direct the jury on the dangers of identification evidence
— honest witnesses can be mistaken. The ADVOKATE factors (and equivalents) must be considered.
Why It Matters: ADVOKATE your witness in their statement. Amount of time observed, Distance, Visibility,
Obstructions, Known to witness, Any reason to remember, Time elapsed, Errors/discrepancies.

Interviews and Admissions
DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC 1 — Right to Lawyer During Questioning
Facts: Suspect questioned without solicitor; admissions made.
Holding: The Supreme Court reviewed the right to consult a lawyer in custody. While not extending the right
to lawyer presence at interview as a constitutional rule, the Court emphasised the importance of access to legal
advice before questioning.
Why It Matters: Pause questioning while solicitor consultation is taking place. Document times: consultation
requested, consultation commenced, consultation ended, questioning resumed.
DPP v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17 — Pre-Interview Legal Advice
Facts: Suspects interviewed before solicitor consultation completed.
Holding: Admissions made before a requested solicitor consultation has been provided are excluded. The right
of access to a lawyer activates upon arrest and request.
Why It Matters: If solicitor requested, do not commence interview until consultation has occurred. Record the
request, the contact, the consultation.
DPP v Brennan [1998] 4 IR 67 — Recent Possession Inference
Facts: Stolen property found on accused; no satisfactory explanation.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Holding: Recent unexplained possession of stolen property founds an inference of theft or handling. The
strength of inference depends on time gap and plausibility of explanation.
Why It Matters: Document the time gap precisely. Question the suspect about provenance: where, when, from
whom, what paid. Note inconsistencies.

Theft and Fraud Offences Act 2001
DPP v Foley [2007] 2 IR 486 — Appropriation and Intent to Deprive
Facts: Theft prosecution; accused disputed mens rea.
Holding: Appropriation = any adverse interference with the proprietary rights of the owner. Intent to
permanently deprive may be inferred from conduct (concealment, denial, disposal, treating-as-own).
Why It Matters: You do not need a confession to mens rea. Conduct grounds the inference.
DPP v Bowe [2017] IECA 215 — Continuing Appropriation
Facts: Shop theft case — dispute over whether offence complete at concealment or only at exit.
Holding: Theft is complete at the point of appropriation with the relevant mens rea — typically at concealment.
Passing the last point of payment is evidential but not legally required.
Why It Matters: If the loss prevention officer observed selection, concealment, and intent, the offence is
complete. Wait for exit if the retailer wishes (good practice), but know the legal position.
DPP v Cleary [2007] IECCA — Intention to Permanently Deprive
Facts: Property taken with eventual return contemplated; dispute over mens rea.
Holding: S.4(5) extends “permanent deprivation” to include borrowing that effectively eliminates the owner’s
value or utility in the property.
Why It Matters: Pawning, prolonged retention, and dispositions that strip value all engage permanentdeprivation reasoning even without literal permanence.
DPP v Morrissey [1982] ILRM 487 — Dishonesty
Facts: Defence of honest belief in entitlement.
Holding: Dishonesty is a jury question, applying ordinary standards. Genuine claim of right made in good faith
is a defence (s.2(1) CJ(T&FO)A 2001).
Why It Matters: Anticipate the claim-of-right defence. Question early. Document the suspect’s state of
awareness as to ownership.

Burglary
DPP v Quilligan [1986] IR 495 — Trespass Element
Facts: Burglary prosecution; element of entry as trespasser disputed.
Holding: Entry as a trespasser requires entry without permission, or entry that exceeds the scope of any
permission given (e.g. permission to enter the shop floor does not extend to staff areas).
Why It Matters: Crucial for retail burglary cases where suspect entered shop floor lawfully but pushed into
staff-only areas. Photograph the access point and signage; statement from store manager on the access scope.
DPP v MacEoin [1978] IR 27 — Building or Part of a Building
Holding: “Building” in burglary context interpreted broadly. “Part of a building” engages where the accused
was lawfully in one part but trespassed into another.
Why It Matters: Particularly relevant for staff-only areas, hotel guest rooms (you may be a guest in the lobby
but trespasser in another guest’s room), and segregated commercial premises.

Part B — European Court of Human Rights (Strasbourg)
The ECHR is given direct effect in Irish law by the European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003. Irish
courts must interpret legislation, so far as possible, consistently with Convention rights. Strasbourg authorities

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

are directly relevant to property crime investigation in arrest, custody, interview, search, and fair-trial contexts.
Salduz v Turkey (Application 36391/02) [GC] 27 November 2008 — Right to Lawyer in Custody
Facts: Applicant interrogated without lawyer; admissions used at trial.
Holding: Article 6 (fair trial) violated. Access to a lawyer should be provided as a rule from the first
interrogation by police, unless compelling reasons restrict it. Admissions made without legal access are
inadmissible against the accused.
Significance: Foundational European authority on custody legal access. Underpins the Irish position in Doyle
and Gormley.
Why It Matters: Do not commence interview before the solicitor consultation has occurred (if requested).
Document the chain meticulously.
Murray (John) v UK (Application 18731/91) 8 February 1996 — Inferences from Silence
Facts: Applicant relied on silence; inferences drawn at trial.
Holding: Drawing inferences from silence is not automatically a violation of Article 6, provided (a) the
conviction is not solely or mainly based on the inference and (b) the warning is properly given.
Significance: Underpins the inference warning regime in s.18 / s.19 / s.19A of the CJA 1984 (as amended).
Why It Matters: When invoking inference provisions, follow the precise statutory wording, ensure the warning
is recorded, ensure the consultation right is satisfied. Imperfect warnings sink the inference.
Saunders v UK (Application 19187/91) 17 December 1996 — Privilege Against Self-Incrimination
Facts: Compelled statements (under DTI inspection powers) used at criminal trial.
Holding: Article 6 violated. Statements obtained under compulsion in a non-criminal investigation may not
subsequently be used as evidence at a criminal trial.
Why It Matters: Be cautious about evidence trail from regulatory or civil inquiries (Revenue, financial
regulators, employer audits). Where you receive compelled material, it may not be usable in evidence even if
helpful in investigation.
Funke v France (Application 10828/84) 25 February 1993 — Privilege Against Self-Incrimination
Holding: Compelling a person to produce documents incriminating themselves violates Article 6.
Why It Matters: S.7 CJA 2011 disclosure orders, financial production orders — understand the limits. Where
the order compels self-incrimination, the material may be inadmissible.
Allan v UK (Application 48539/99) 5 November 2002 — Cell Informants
Facts: Police placed an informant in cell with suspect; informant elicited admissions; recording used.
Holding: Article 6 violated. The functional equivalent of interrogation by state agents without warning or legal
access violates the privilege against self-incrimination.
Why It Matters: Be cautious about communications with detained suspects through any state-facing channel
— including civilian volunteers, informants, even other Gardaí in informal settings. Anything elicited may be
excluded.
Khan v UK (Application 35394/97) 12 May 2000 — Covert Surveillance and Privacy
Facts: Covert listening device used to record applicant; product used in evidence.
Holding: Article 8 (private life) violated where surveillance was not adequately governed by domestic law.
However, admission of the evidence did not per se violate Article 6 — trial fairness assessed in the round.
Why It Matters: Covert surveillance must be done strictly within the Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009
framework. Authorisation, recording, and chain are essential.
Bykov v Russia (Application 4378/02) [GC] 10 March 2009 — Surveillance Authorisation
Holding: Surveillance must be governed by clear, accessible, foreseeable domestic law. Without it, Article 8
is violated.
Why It Matters: Reinforces Khan. Always operate inside the Surveillance Act.
Schenk v Switzerland (Application 10862/84) 12 July 1988 — Unlawfully Obtained Evidence

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Holding: Article 6 does not lay down a rule of admissibility — that is a matter for national law. However, the
overall fairness of proceedings must be respected.
Why It Matters: Even if Irish admissibility law (post-JC) allows evidence in, the wider Article 6 fairness
analysis remains a backstop. Build clean evidence.
Beuze v Belgium (Application 71409/10) [GC] 9 November 2018 — Right to Lawyer (Reaffirmed)
Holding: Reaffirmed Salduz. Confirmed that systemic absence of lawyer access during questioning constitutes
Article 6 violation. Trial fairness assessed in the whole-of-proceedings.
Why It Matters: Provides the contemporary articulation of the right. Cited routinely in custody-rights
challenges.
Ibrahim and Others v UK (Application 50541/08) [GC] 13 September 2016 — Safety Interviews
Facts: Police conducted safety interviews of terrorism suspects before lawyer access, on the basis of imminent
threat.
Holding: Compelling reasons may justify a temporary restriction on the Salduz right, but the overall fairness
analysis applies. Statements obtained must not be the sole or decisive evidence at trial.
Why It Matters: Limited and exceptional; not a routine route. For property crime, almost never engaged.
Loizidou v Turkey — Article 1 Protocol 1 (Property)
Article 1 of Protocol 1 protects “peaceful enjoyment of possessions.” Engaged in property-seizure contexts:
ensure seizures are lawful, proportionate, and time-limited. Where property is retained beyond what is
necessary, owner has a Convention right to release.
Why It Matters: Long-retained exhibits without lawful basis are vulnerable. Return property promptly once
evidential purpose served, unless a court order or statutory basis justifies continued retention.
Niemietz v Germany (Application 13710/88) 16 December 1992 — Article 8 and Premises
Holding: Article 8 (private life and home) extends to business premises in some circumstances. Search of a
lawyer’s office without adequate procedural safeguards violated Article 8.
Why It Matters: Search warrants must be properly grounded, even for commercial premises. Document the
basis.

Part C — Court of Justice of the European Union (Luxembourg)
CJEU authority bites in property crime mostly through (a) mutual recognition instruments (European Arrest
Warrant, European Investigation Order), (b) data protection (GDPR and Law Enforcement Directive 2016/680),
and (c) Charter of Fundamental Rights interpretations.
Case C-216/18 PPU Minister for Justice v LM (Celmer) [2018]
Holding: Where there is a real risk of breach of the fundamental right to a fair trial in the issuing Member State,
the executing authority may refuse to surrender under a European Arrest Warrant.
Why It Matters: For organised property crime with cross-border suspects, EAW procedures may be engaged.
Understand the Celmer assessment may delay surrender; investigation must continue in parallel.
Case C-746/18 H.K. v Prokuratuur [2021] — Retained Data Access
Holding: Access to retained communications data for criminal investigation purposes must be subject to prior
review by an independent authority, save in cases of validly established urgency.
Why It Matters: Disclosure orders under the Communications (Retention of Data) Act 2011 must satisfy CJEU
standards. Most data access for property crime now requires judicial authorisation.
Joined Cases C-203/15 and C-698/15 Tele2 Sverige and Watson [2016]
Holding: Bulk retention of communications data is incompatible with EU law unless strictly necessary,
proportionate, and subject to safeguards.
Why It Matters: Background context for any data-access investigation. Practical effect: your DOCB-routed
disclosure orders are paper-trail-heavy for a reason — each must be defensible against CJEU standards.

Part D — Using This Appendix in Practice
These are the cases most likely to surface in the investigation, charging, and trial of property crime. Use the
appendix:
• As a quick refresher before a court appearance — read the relevant block on the train in.
• As a reference when drafting a difficult cover report — cite the authority you are operating under.
• As a coaching aid — colleagues new to property crime can read the summaries to orient.
• As a sanity check before a contentious decision — do my grounds line up with Walsh? Is my warrant route
Damache-safe? Is my interview Salduz-compliant?
Final warning: always verify the live status of any case before relying on it in evidence. The vLex Ireland
database is a reliable starting point; courts.ie and bailii.org carry full judgments; the Irish Reports and Irish Law
Reports Monthly remain the formal print citation sources.

Statutory Anchors
The principal statutes engaged across this volume:
• Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 — the central property-crime code. Theft (s.4),
burglary (s.12-13), robbery (s.14), articles for use (s.15), handling (s.17), possession (s.18), deception (s.6),
false accounting (s.10), forgery and false instruments (s.25-26), unlawful use of computer (s.9).
• Criminal Damage Act 1991 — criminal damage (s.2(1)), damage endangering life (s.2(2)), arson (s.2(3)),
threats (s.3), articles (s.4), defences (s.6).
• Road Traffic Act 1961 — UTMV (s.112), passenger variant. Dangerous / drink-drive provisions (crossreference Vol 7).
• Criminal Justice Act 1984 — arrestable offences regime (s.4), search on arrest (s.6), inference provisions
(s.18, s.19, s.19A).
• Criminal Justice Act 2006 — search warrants for arrestable offences (s.10).
• Criminal Justice Act 2007 — organised-crime detention (s.50).
• Criminal Procedure Act 2010 — various procedural provisions.
• Garda Síochána Act 2005 — Code of Ethics enabling provision (s.17).
• European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003 — Convention rights in domestic law.
• Firearms and Offensive Weapons Act 1990 — production of article in public place (s.11), other weapon
offences.
• Criminal Justice (Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing) Act 2010 — money laundering (s.7);
engaged in fraud / handling cases.
• Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 — assault offences relevant to robbery contexts (Vol 2
primarily).

Closing Note — Volume 1
This volume covers the seven principal property offence categories and their operational investigation. Every
chapter follows the same structure to support both forward learning and reverse reference in live use.
The Two-Track Approach (Live / Post-Fact) frames every investigation; the Garda Decision-Making Model
documents the reasoning; the Garda Code of Ethics governs the conduct; the Defence Will Attack section
anticipates the courtroom challenge; the Case Law Appendix supplies the authority.
Volume 2 (Crimes Against Person) and Volume 4 (Domestic Violence) follow. Volume 9 (Cross-Cutting
Reference) collects the standalone chapters on Digital Evidence, Suspect Interview, Witness Handling, Scene
Preservation, Court / Cross-Examination, Clocks & Deadlines, Probationer’s First File, Juvenile / Diversion,
Red Flag Escalation, the Golden Hour Checklist, and the consolidated Mnemonics.
Final reminder: this is a working reference, not legal advice. Verify live statute and current authority before
relying in evidence or court.

How to Use This Volume
Volume 2 covers crimes against the person — offences that injure, threaten, restrain, or kill. The investigative
posture differs from property crime in a fundamental way: a human being is the scene. Medical priority, forensic
on the body, witness reliability under trauma, and the victim’s ongoing safety run through every chapter.
Structure:
• Chapter 1 — Homicide: Murder and Manslaughter
• Chapter 2 — Assault (s.2, s.3, s.4 NFOAP 1997)
• Chapter 3 — Threats, Harassment, Coercion, False Imprisonment
• Chapter 4 — Endangerment, Poisoning, Assault on Garda
Each incident follows the same 14-block structure as Volume 1.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The Two-Track Approach — Adapted for Crimes
Against Person
Every incident is either Track A (live / just-occurred) or Track B (reported after the fact). For person-crime
there is an additional dimension overlaying both: the victim’s ongoing safety.

Track A — Live
Priorities: preserve life, preserve scene, preserve evidence, secure suspect, identify witnesses, manage IP
welfare. Medical assessment overrides all other actions.

Track B — Post-Fact
Priorities: medical follow-up, photograph injuries while visible, retrieve hospital records, secure
communications, race the CCTV overwrite clock, lock in IP account.

The Overlay — Ongoing Safety
In DV, harassment, threats — any case where the suspect knows the IP — the IP’s safety from the moment of
reporting is a Garda obligation. Bail conditions, protection orders, safety planning, victim liaison — these run
alongside the criminal investigation.
KEY POINT — LIFE BEFORE EVIDENCE
If a choice presents between preserving evidence and preserving life or preventing serious harm, life and harmprevention always win.
Medical attention for the IP is not negotiated. Evidence may be partially lost; that is a manageable cost.
The Garda who calls the ambulance first is doing the right thing.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Medical and Forensic Priorities
Medical — Always First
1. Treat injury — first aid, ambulance call, hospital transport.
2. Note injuries in own observation, IP’s description, medical staff documentation.
3. Photograph injuries with IP consent — at scene, at hospital, again at 48 hours when bruising develops.
4. Hospital records — obtain through s.10 CJA 2006 warrant where IP unable / unwilling, or by consent
disclosure.
5. Statement of treating medical professional — GP, A&E, paramedic — mechanism, injuries observed,
treatment given.

Forensic — On the Body
• Clothing — IP’s clothing potentially evidential (suspect DNA, blood, fibres); preserve.
• Swabs — sexual offences / serious assault via FME at Rotunda SATU.
• DNA / blood from suspect on IP and vice versa; SOCO management.
• Toxicology where intoxication in issue.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Garda Code of Ethics — Applied to Crimes Against
Person
Volume 2 brings the Code into direct contact with vulnerable human beings — injured, frightened, bereaved
— at the most acute moments of their lives. Every standard is salient. Three are paramount.

Std 4 — Respect and Equality
Person-crime investigation reveals every Garda’s underlying disposition. The intoxicated IP, the homeless IP,
the IP with a criminal record, the IP whose lifestyle is unconventional, the suspect who is articulate and wellpresented, the suspect who is unwashed and incoherent — each must receive the same investigative depth and
the same evidential rigour. The Code does not permit “deserving” and “undeserving” victims.

Std 1 — Duty to Persons in Custody
Suspects in person-crime cases often present with complex needs: trauma, intoxication, mental illness, suicide
risk. They are more, not less, deserving of the safeguards. Medical assessment on arrival mandatory in serious
cases; rest documented; suicide watch where indicated; appropriate adult where required; pause questioning
when distress is acute.

Std 7 — Speaking up and Reporting Wrongdoing
Person-crime brings stress, fatigue, and emotional pressure. A colleague short of patience with a difficult IP, a
senior officer pressing for a particular outcome, a corner being cut on disclosure — these moments arrive. The
Code obligation is to speak up. The institutional cost of speaking up is far lower than the institutional cost of a
quashed conviction.

Compliment Points in Person Crime
Recurring categories across Volume 2:
• First-aid intervention — pressure on wound, CPR, airway, recovery position before paramedics. Always
written up.
• De-escalation under risk — talking an armed / distressed suspect into safe surrender. Commendation
submission.
• Victim engagement — building trust with frightened or traumatised IP, enabling workable statement.
• Family Liaison — carrying a bereaved family through the criminal-process months.
• Honest reporting of error — reporting own procedural error early; Std 8 in action.
